And I would ask that you would indulge me for just a little bit as I go over the foundation of what it is that we're going to talk about today, and why this is so critical and why this is so valuable, even if you think you know it. Now, you've heard, and it's been long known that the priority order of impact, when it comes to marketing, when it comes to producing sales, when it comes to conversions in the world of direct response is list. First, in other words, the priority in conversions is the audience is making sure that you're talking to the right people. The reality is, no matter how much else you know about marketing, no matter what your skill sets are in every other area of marketing from whether it be copy and headline creation big idea or a unique mechanism campaign arguments, offer creation for that matter, you know, copy, with bank copy sales technique, no matter what your skill sets are, if you're talking to the wrong people, or a poorly targeted list, your conversion rate is going to suffer. Right, you could have the best product on like I've said this for a long time on mixed martial arts, right, and if you're talking to elderly grandmothers and grandfathers, there's a strong likelihood that you're not going to sell a whole lot of them, no matter how great your offer is no matter how great your copy is no matter how great your marketing is no matter what tech you're using, no matter if you're using the latest with bang strategy or tactic out there. And so the priority is first and foremost audience, but after audience is the offer, and after the offer comes the copy. Now, a lot of you may may have heard this already, you may know this right this is a long established kind of principle rule in the world of direct response. But here is the reality. The reality is is that this is not the way the overwhelming majority of marketers and entrepreneurs online operate when it comes to campaign creation, right the majority of marketers and entrepreneurs online, when it comes to their funnels and their campaigns, they're pouring a tremendous amount of time and effort into the marketing and the copy side, they're dialing in, or trying to dial in every single word of their copy, their VSL script their long form sales letter, they're working tirelessly to dial in every sentence and every paragraph right and then they're hemming and hauling over, should I go with this kind of funnel or this model, should I use this model should I use a VSL should I use a webinar should it be an evergreen webinar should it be a live webinar should I do this should I have six steps in my campaign or should I have five steps in my campaign, thinking that the magic is really in the marketing and the copy side, but the reality is is that the offer is a much bigger needle mover in direct response marketing than is the copy. Yet, the overwhelming majority of entrepreneurs and marketers, maybe some of you on here, pour your heart and soul into the copy and marketing side, and almost treat the offer like a secondary, a secondary thought, I would say that there are, I would say the large majority of online entrepreneurs, they probably spend less than 60 minutes, putting together thinking about and putting together their offer, many people, right, even on here, likely spend less than 30 minutes, contemplating thinking about and engineering their offer, not realizing that the offer is a bigger needle mover, when it comes to conversions, then is copy. Right. Now, the other thing that I want you to really understand that I really want you to grasp here, and this is critical. And that is what I would say is, when I said earlier that there's nothing more valuable that you can learn today, right today, then what it is that we're talking about here. And, you know, it's, it's largely, there's a lot of factors that are contributing to that but largely driven by this, that most entrepreneurs, most marketers are trying to compete on better copy and better marketing. They're trying to compete for, for sales, for customers, for buyers, for lifetime value growth. They're trying to compete by having better copy and better marketing. And here is the thing that I want you to understand. It kills me to say this. It pains me to say this, but you need to hear it because it's the truth. The reality is, is that trying to compete on better marketing is brutally difficult. It's the most difficult approach to competing in a crowded market. It is the most difficult approach because, you know, look, writing copy coming up with great big marketing ideas are not easy. These are not easy steps. These are not easy tasks. They can oftentimes be tedious. They require a lot of, a lot of research, a lot of examination. They require a strong nomination. They require a strong finger on the pulse of your market. They're difficult to execute even for people that have been around the game for a significant length of time. Right. The reality is, as you're going to learn today is that you ideally do not want to try to compete on better copy and better marketing, especially in crowded markets, especially in competitive markets, especially in saturated markets. Like, look, the reality is, is that right? There are some great marketers out there. And the last thing that you want to do is try to go head to head with them on a difficult skill set to execute. The beauty is that with a great offer, with a winning offer, you can get away with average copy and an average marketing campaign. Right. The reality is that you can get away with average copy and an average marketing campaign when you've got a stellar offer and offer that I'm going to teach you how to put together today and offer like we're going to describe, look at, dissect and lay out for you today. You can get away with average copy and right and an average campaign. But man, when you've got an average offer, even great copy and great marketing will not make up for that. Right. A weak offer, a run of the mill offer is very difficult to make work, even with great copy and great marketing. And so you don't want to try to compete with better marketing. You want to compete with a better offer. This is not to say that you don't want the best marketing and the best copy possible. That's not what I'm saying. I am saying you do want good copy. You do want a properly structured and strategically engineered marketing campaign, but you really want that right to communicate. You want to use those things to communicate a winning offer. When you do, when you combine a winning offer with even good copy, like, you know, average copy and a good marketing strategy, man, skies the limit for you. You will dominate. What you see from the majority of marketers in the online marketing space are run of the mill offers that are trying to be propped up with hyperbolic exaggerated copy and claims and just ridiculous promises. That's not how you want to want to compete. And so number one, what you have to understand is that better offers or a better offer, a winning offer makes marketing and selling easy. Makes marketing and selling easy. Here's an example that I've used for years. And I want you to think about this for one second. Okay. I want you to imagine that I ask you guys collectively as a as a group. I ask you to help me sell these two vehicles. So on the left, we've got a beat up rusted out old VW truck. Right. Let's say it's got an AM FM radio, the interiors are all torn up banged up. I'm not even sure if this thing starts on the right. We've got a gorgeous brand new call it gunmetal. Matt black Aston Martin call it a $350,000 vehicle ish. Give or take. Right. And now I ask you, I say for you guys, I want you to sell each of these vehicles for me for $45,000 a pop. So that banged up VW. Right. And on the right, a brand new Aston Martin, and I want you to sell each for $45,000. Well, let me let me be crystal clear if you don't see it already to sell that banged up VW truck to for $45,000. You better have some serious marketing chops, you better have some serious copy chops, you better have some serious sales chops to sell that vehicle for you. Let's just let's just assume that that's not anything that is in high demand as a classic vehicle I have no idea I'm not into vehicles. And so right just assuming that it's a run of the mill banged up beat up VW truck, you got to have some mad chops to to sell that vehicle. I want to ask you something. Do you need to have incredible marketing skills, copy chops, sales skills to sell a $300 plus $1000 Aston Martin for $45,000. The answer is absolutely not your biggest obstacle would be convincing people it's not stolen. Right, that that it's legit. Right. And so you don't need massive marketing chops incredible marketing chops incredible copy skills are incredible sales skills. When you have that kind of offer. This is an extreme example. Yes, and that is based purely on discount right or you know the value exchange here. But the thing that I want you to understand, nonetheless, is that when you've got a great offer, you don't need extensive copy skills or marketing chops. Because the offer is so good that it does the heavy lifting. See when the offer is run of the mill, when the offer is average, when the offer is sub par, when the offer isn't irresistible, as most people like to talk about. Well, then you got to prop it up with great copy you got to prop it up with great marketing you got to prop it up with extensive follow up you got to prop it up with so much so many, you know, so many, so many tactics and strategies, and all that. And you get when you have a great offer, the reality is a stellar offer. Let me let me kind of just jump ahead for one second and say this, that a stellar offer. The reality is is that you don't need a big marketing idea. You often don't even need a unique mechanism, not saying that you wouldn't want to use a unique mechanism, but a great offer, you can run ads for the offer. Right. And you could bring people to a very extremely direct campaign that is about the offer, and you will sell like crazy. You have a great, a great offer, a winning offer. When you don't, then we need a lot of these tactics, a lot of these whiz bang techniques to prop it up to get attention to try to build value to try to, you know, create this wizardry. And so, right, great offers, better offers make marketing and selling so excuse me, so much easier, better offers as I already said, a better offer will always be better marketing, a better offer will always be better copy, right. And here is Gary Halbert, arguably one of the most respected legends in the world of direct response. This was a guy who was a copy guy, one of the greats no longer with us, unfortunately, but one of the greats, a guy who talked most extensively about copy, a guy who was an unbelievable copywriter, and he said strong copy will not overcome a weak offer, but a strong offer will succeed in spite of weak copy written by marketing morons. Right. Like, right, like written a strong offer will succeed in spite of weak copy, weak copy written by a marketing moron. There's no marketing morons on here. So you're not even we're not even talking about starting out this low. The point is, is that a great offer will compensate for weak copy or average copy and an average marketing campaign, but great marketing campaigns and great copy will rarely compensate for a subpar average offer. The reality is, is that today, in most competitive markets, in most competitive markets where there are, you know, like super competitive markets and when I say super competitive, I mean, from a direct response perspective, there are a lot of funnels there are a lot of campaigns there are a lot of advertisers, there's just a lot of offers, you know, being put out there. The reality is, is that a winning offer will allow you to dominate in those kind of markets will allow you to dominate the likelihood in these super crowded super saturated markets, the likelihood of you dominating based on better copy or better marketing is slim to none. You don't want to fight that battle. You want to change your frame and recognize the power of a better offer, the power of a winning offer, the power of constructing a stellar offer for your marketplace. And that's why I say that today, especially creating better offers is the supreme skill, right, it is the most valuable skill in all of marketing today. I really believe that it's always been right but it's more important, more critical today than ever before. And this is why I say and this is the first kind of mindset shift or really the only mindset shift in the foundations section that I just want to give you is that what I want you to begin thinking like or thinking as right is instead of first thinking of yourself as a marketer, like instead of thinking like I'm a marketer I do marketing. I want you to above and before all else. I want you to view yourself and act like and conduct yourself like you are first and foremost, an offer creator, you are an offer creator. So you're going to you're going to see in just a little bit and this is an important distinction that I want you to that I want you to grasp that before anything else, before you think about a big marketing idea, before you write a headline, before you try to come up with subheads before you start to think about any body copy for your campaign before you start thinking about what type of funnel model should I use before you start thinking about what upsells should I have. I want you to think, think like act like function like an offer creator first and foremost, and I want you to put your emphasis there using what it is that I'm going to teach you today. I, in my old office in the old house for years, I had right next to my, the, there was a whiteboard in front of my desk or off to the right of my desk. And on that whiteboard, a lot of things got put up on that whiteboard and a lot of things came down. But one thing that never came down that was up there for years for me to see out of the right side of my eye every single day when I sat down at my desk was focus on crafting killer offers focus on crafting killer offers first and foremost, you will make everything else infinitely easier when you start here. And this is why, like I just said, and that's why there's a star in the upper right corner of this little slide, the offer should be engineered before you work on a single part of your marketing campaign. This is why in the E five acquisition accelerator in the E five masterclass before we do any, any other part of the campaign, we start with the offer, we start with the offer because it's the ultimate destination of where we're bringing prospects. It's the ultimate conversion tool in every campaign and everything else is there to support the offer everything else is there to ultimately bring the prospect to the offer with the right, the right frame. So, what is an offer I know that this might seem like totally basic, basic, but I want you to understand a couple of a couple of nuances here. So, an offer in the direct response world is what the prospect gets. And what they need to do to get it. Now, you'll notice that there are two parts to this. What the prospect gets, and what they need to do to get it. Now, what most marketers think or believe when they hear this or hear a definition like this is, well, what the prospect gets is the product and bonuses maybe, right. And what they need to do to get it is fill out an online form and pay X amount of money. But it really, as you're going to understand today goes far deeper than just that. That's the superficial what the prospect gets and what they need to do to get it. I want you to recognize that there is a layer deeper and that deeper layer needs to be taken into consideration when we are engineering your winning offer. So, there are there is, what is the prospect get prospect gets. They get the results and benefits. First and foremost, what I want you to understand is that the what the prospect gets, and the thing that is ultimately most important to them and why there's a star or an asterisk next to this is the results and benefits the pay off the transformation to change the alleviation of pain, the the accomplishment or experience of an unfulfilled desire. That's what they care about most. And so when we say what does the prospect get well first and foremost what's most important to the prospect is the result, the outcome to change the transformation. So when you're thinking about what is C C and let me tell you why this is so important and why I put this slide in so early, right, is that what most what what happens with most entrepreneurs with most marketers is that they put together some sort of campaign, whether it's a marketing campaign or a sales campaign or a VSL or a long form sales that are a one. Webinar or whatever. And what happens is when they go into offer presentation mode when they present their offer to the prospect, because they think that. Okay, the offer is what the prospect gets and what they need to do to get it and they think that what the prospect gets is the product, right and the bonuses and what they need to do the prospect in order to get that is the fill out the order form and pay X amount of dollars. They end up presenting their offer in a way that lacks emotional punch right there. They're talking almost about the features of the offer in general, and they're not talking about what the prospect cares about most. See, you have to recognize listen to me, all of you, right, nobody buys a product or service for the product or service itself. People buy products and services for the result that they believe they're going to get from it. That's what they care about. They don't care, right, they care about the result. First and foremost, that's the thing. They don't care about whether it like it whether it comes with one widget or three widgets or five widgets, right, what they care about is the result. And so the point is that right people don't buy financial newsletters because they're excited about eight pages of text showing up in their mailbox. They invest in a financial newsletter because they want the more money in their portfolio. They want more profitable trades they want to make more money and have more money they want to gain financial freedom. Right. And so nobody joins a gym, merely because it's got cardio equipment in there. They join the gym for what they believe the cardio equipment is ultimately going to do for them, what they can experience from the from the cardio equipment. And that's the same thing. Nobody buys a bottle of let's say vitamin D because they're thrilled about having 100 tablets in some, you know, opaque bottle in their kitchen cabinet. No, they're buying the vitamin D for what it is that they believe that vitamin D is going to do for them, right, that it's going to strengthen their bones. It's going to improve their cardiovascular system. And so you have to realize in this context, when we're talking about what does the prospect get first and foremost, the thing that I care about most in terms of being an offer creator in terms of being a marketer is demonstrating the results, the benefits, the transformation, the alleviation of pain, because that's why they buy. And then of course, there is the product, right, the bonuses and usually the product, the service, the bonuses are linked to those results and those benefits. And I'm going to show you this and explain how we do this and explain the whole process for presenting it the right way. But the other thing that I want you to see here is that is that the prospect also gets reassurance, right, they get a guarantee. See, you have to understand that right like, what is the value, the benefit, if you will, to the prospect with a guarantee when a guarantee is used in an offer. Well, it reduces risk, right, it demonstrates that like you can have more confidence moving forward with the purchase because you're not risking money or you're not risking time or you're not risking effort, right. And so you have to understand that part of what they're getting in the offer, right, is the results, the outcomes, right, more confidence, lower risk, as well as products like the bonuses and all the things that they deliver and do for them, and all the pay off. That's what the prospect gets. Now what does the prospect need to do what what what do they do in order to get this. Well, you have to realize that there's a number of things here included. When we say what do they need to do to get it. What do we mean by it. Right. Well, we're not just saying when I say it. I'm not just talking about what do they need to do to get the offer right what do they need to do to take advantage of the offer. Ultimately, what we're saying is, what do they need to do to get it to get the value, the payoff, the results from the offer. Right. Remember, this is a slight nuance, but I want you to get this. What do they need to do to get it it being the value the payoff the results the transformation the things that they want from the offer. Well, of course, right, they need to pay for it. Right. There are certain risks that they might take they might have to write what do they need to do they might have to risk their time. They might have to risk their their their risking their money. They might have to risk potential embarrassment or humiliation if they're you know if this is a, you know if they're buying something for their company if they're a buyer right and they have a direct report to answer to. But what do they also need to do, often depending on the product that you're selling or the service that you're selling there might be work and effort involved. You're going to see in a little bit why this is so important, especially like these last three right here, you're going to understand why this is so critical. Right. What do they need to do to get it well how much work or effort is involved. Right. Is this something that they're going to need to invest five hours a day for the next 10 weeks to reap the rewards. Or is it something that they push a button and they've got the rewards. And so how much work, how much effort, how much energy, how much time and how much discomfort, do they have to endure in order to get it. These are all really important things. This slide alone. And what it is that I just shared with you should should already begin to shape your understanding of when we're talking about the offer, we're not just talking about payment and filling out an order form and getting some products and bonuses. We're talking about what they get in terms of the payoff the results through the products through the services, and the additional things like the guarantee the reduction of risk the increased confidence. And what do they need to do in order to get it. Of course, we know they need to pay right by paying there are certain certain risks involved. But then what do they need to do to actually call it use the product to actually reap the rewards in terms of work, effort, energy, time and discomfort. This is really important. Now ultimately what we are trying to do what we ultimately want to do here is we we want to tilt the scales in terms of the value proposition in favor of what the prospect gets. Another, another words right that the what the prospect gets is far more valuable than what the prospect needs to do or what the prospect does. And so we're going to demonstrate right that the value of what the prospect gets, including results, right because that's where the real value is. Understand the real value. If you sell some sort of information product, coaching consulting or something like that, the real value is not in 20 videos, right in Q&A calls in like in whatever else you give them the value is in what those things do for them. So ultimately what we're trying to demonstrate to the prospect is that what you get is far more valuable than what you do or give to get it. That's ultimately what we are doing. Now I want you to understand, based on what it is that I just shared with you that the product is not the offer. The product is not the offer. The product, what we call the deliverables, right and we're going to talk about that, the deliverables are not the offer, right. They're not the offer, right, the product is not the offer. The product is a part of the offer. The product is a component of the offer, but it's not the offer. It's one part of the offer. See, ultimately, I want you to get this, that what we're doing is we're selling the prospect on the value of the whole offer, not just the product or service. We're selling them, right. See, understand when you include things like let's say we're going to talk about this in a little bit. So I'm jumping ahead, but when you've got let's say, you know, you've got a soft offer where it's like, pay nothing today, enjoy the, you know, use the software for the next 30 days and only, you know, if you're blown away by it and head over heels thrilled would you pay. And you also get these, these bonuses, right, and excuse me, and, and even if you don't love it, send one email you'll be able to keep the bonuses, and then you've got six months to keep using it and it either improves your production, you know, your productivity or I'm going to give you your money back plus, you know, another 100 bucks or something like that, right. What you're ultimately selling them on is the value of that entire offer, not just the product, because there's value and benefits to each of those little things that I just rattled off there. There's value and benefit to the bonuses, there's value and benefit to being able to keep the bonuses, even if you don't like it. There's value and benefit to being able to experience the software in this example, before you, you pay for it. There's value and benefit to then knowing that you can use it for the next six months and you either see results or you get your money back plus an extra 100 bucks. See, we're getting buy in on the value proposition based on the whole offer, not just the product. And so I want you to begin another little mindset shift here. I want you to begin to view the offer as the quote unquote product. So up to this point, many of you have likely been thinking like that the whole the core the 80% of the offer is the product, right, like what they get, you know what I mean they get a bunch of videos they get a bunch of Q&As they get recordings they get access to this they got a Facebook group they get all these things you've been thinking that right ultimately that that product makes up the bulk of the offer. I really want you to begin to look at the offer as a whole, and all of the pieces that we cover today as the product that you are marketing and selling we're marketing and selling prospects on the offer, not the product, right, we're ultimately marketing and selling them on the value of the offer. When we go into offer presentation mode, we are getting buy in on the entire offer, not just the product. Now look, and I've been asked this question before let me take a drink. If not, man, I will lose my voice quickly. That's what happens now after all these years after 20 plus years doing this man, the voice is getting weaker. You know I've been asked before like well what do you do man how do you compete if you're selling the same exact product as let's say somebody up the street. Right, and where does this question come from, right this question comes from the thought, the perspective that well, I'm competing on the product the product is the offer therefore how do I compete. If you know the product is not the offer, the product is only a piece of the offer. So let's say we were selling this, the same grill as a store up the road, and let's say the store up the road, if they're like the typical entrepreneur the typical entrepreneur, they're selling the grill, you know, what do you get you get the grill, it's 3900 bucks, and you have a 14 day guarantee, right like and that's what do most people, what do most entrepreneurs do, right they'll either go with the same offer, or what will they, what will they attempt to do, what do you think the average entrepreneur, the typical entrepreneur doesn't know what you guys know, right what do you think they would do to try to compete here, what is the number one move exactly, you got it, Dion, Sarkis, you got it, they would try to discount, what they would do is they would lower their price, right, and they end up eating away at their margin, and come trying to compete on low price is a race to the bottom. And so, right is a race to the bottom, just ask Kmart what happens when you try to when you try to be the lowest price provider, and then somebody else comes along like Walmart, and eats your lunch. Right and so not only is that business brutally difficult when you're trying to compete on price because you've got weaker margins, right but you run the risk of somebody else coming along, having a different business model and just saying, you know what we sell a lot of other things, besides just grills. And so we're willing to break even on the grill, like I'll break even on the grill I'll sell it at cost I'll sell for 1500 bucks and crush everybody else. Right if that's, you know, like what happens, you don't want to do that. And so then we've got this other option, right, and the other option, like Mitchell just said Mitchell kudos man you're absolutely right. So we can start to improve the offer. So we can say you get the grill, you get a free set of brand new barbecue utensils. We have a whole online course on grilling mastery that you get access to for life. And you get a free free GLEE grill cleaner. We're gonna, you get free shipping and set up so you don't even have to lug this bad boy out of the store, we're going to deliver it to you and for you, and then we're going to set this bad boy up and you get a one year guarantee and it's only, you know, 650, six payments to 650. This is a wildly different offer. Same product, right same product ultimately, but a wildly different offer. Now the reality is and I want you to understand just going off script, so to speak for a second. The reality is, is that I could probably even charge more, I could probably charge an extra hundred bucks, right on this offer, make it a cool $4,000 on this bad boy, so that I'm not absorbing any additional costs right so in other words I'll take the cost of the free GLEE utensils, the free grilling videos which is really no cost, the free grill cleaner and the shipping and set up. I could add an additional hundred bucks to it, right, to just compensate for those things. And we would probably still win, right, like we would probably still win, because it's hard to say, well for an extra hundred bucks, man I get delivery I get set up I get barbecue utensils I get grilled grilling videos I get grill cleaner, like I get a one year guarantee on this bad boy like it's hard to you know to say no for just an extra hundred bucks for that. The point that I'm trying to share with you here, right, is that you could have the same product and you could have a wildly different offer. And the quality of the offer will have a radical impact on how well that boy that bad boy sells. Also, a radical impact on what you can charge, because with the right offer right if somebody is, if somebody is, let me take a step back and share something off the wall. So, so my COO and I named Damian been a lifelong friend I've known him since he was like 16 years old. So, we dabble in some real estate. And we were looking at a property, I don't know, it was like 179 grand or something like that. And there were, there were offers already submitted, right to the, to the owner, there were offers submitted, and there were a handful of offers that were above asking. And so, but we knew that the overwhelming majority of those offers that were submitted were were traditional mortgage, you know, based offers. And so, we were going to pay cash. And so, I said, go in and and put in an offer for 173, let them know that we'll pay cash, and we can close as soon as they want. So there's no bank stuff, there's no delay, there's nothing, right, we'll pay cash, and we can close as quickly as they want. That's a radically different offer. See, the other elements change. I'm sharing that in this context, because what I want you to understand is that there are ways that you can, you can increase the price point, like you can increase the price point. If you look at this, um, no, so this isn't right, like we could have six payments of 700, right, which is more than what it is that option number one is charging, but we have a more attractive offer, because they're paying less today, they're getting all these things free shipping, and and not the whole point is this not to be labor this point, but the product is just a piece of the offer with a better offer you can become wildly more attractive to the same marketplace, even increasing price point. Every direct response marketing campaign has an offer. And this is kind of we're going to wrap this up this little section and start to get into the your winning offer process. Every direct response marketing campaign has an offer. What I want you to realize I want you to think of this right I want you to recognize this and I'm sharing this because I know that there are likely some people on here that let's say, are doing some sort of telephone call right so at the end of the call to action might be to jump on the phone, right and ultimately maybe you're selling on the phone, right. And so you're not selling a product at the end of your marketing campaign you're ultimately quote unquote selling on an offer to jump on a phone, but it's an offer nonetheless, a lead magnet right is an offer. There are, right, what does the prospect get right and what do they need to do to get it that's still an offer, just because there is no financial exchange doesn't change the fact that it's an offer, because when we get to the, the the components of a proposition the proposition portion of an offer, you're going to see that there's price the price might be zero, but that doesn't change whether it's an offer or not. And so a lead magnet right is an offer. So there is still, what does the prospect get and what do they need to do to get it. I should really say here when it comes to moving prospects to act. It takes extreme extraordinary measures to compel people to act.
