And so you got to recognize that if you're not willing to like you got to recognize that like you got to be willing to do the work necessary to have marketing to have an offer that is nailed right it can change your life. Right, it's not cliche to say that one great offer for one marketing campaign can change your life forever. That's not cliche that's not hype that's not exaggeration. That's the reality, but you've got to be willing to do the work necessary to have it, which brings me to this first piece this first chunk that we need to talk about, which is the examination stage the examination stage for us right is the first stage in the E five method. I'm not going to get into the full examination or research from the E five method. I'm going to keep it specific to offer engineering, but you need to recognize that right examination, you could just use the word research if you want. It's a little different. The reason why I call it examination is because it's not really research it's really more analysis than anything that you're going to see, but the research stage is where the money is made in marketing in offer creation the research stage is where the money is made. And that's because we're going to really ultimately in when it when it comes to examination. There are three things that we look at. And when I said to you earlier right that, you know you create you engineer your offer before you work on a single piece of your marketing campaign. Well, the reality is, is that you really want to do the examination before you do anything else. So the truth is that even before you do you sit down to engineer an offer. You really need to do the examination that I'm going to explain to you, because without the examination that it becomes very difficult to develop a winning offer like I'm going to show you today because you're operating absent of information necessary to make certain decisions. See the beauty is, is that right like the gold that you need the insight that you need in order to be able to craft a winning offer and ultimately a winning marketing campaign is found and unearthed during the research. It's not in your head. Right. It's not like on a blank piece of paper or blank word document. It is out there through the, um, through the research. So there are three things that we always examine before we create a marketing campaign. I'm going to tell you the two that we're going to talk about for offer creation. So let me first give you the three. So the first one is the prospect. Obviously this is the most important category of examination and research like right we started out our time together today earlier talking about the number one priority in order of impact on conversions being the audience. Well, it's not just that we are talking to the right audience, but it's that we know that audience. We know what makes them tick. We understand their problem, the problem that they're trying to solve. We understand the issues that that problem is causing them. Right. We understand their, um, their wants and their desires as it relates to solving the problem. We understand their emotions and feelings. We understand their, their belief. We understand what offers they've seen. And I'm going to cover this in just a little bit, but it all begins with your prospect because how do you, how do you create a wildly compelling offer and offer that is a no brainer for a prospect to say yes to if you don't know the prospect. Right. If I just said to you, I want you to come up with a wildly compelling offer for a market and I didn't tell you who the market was. How do you come up with a, um, how do you come up with an offer? Or if I told you what the market was, but you knew nothing about the prospect. How do you come up with a compelling offer? Right. You, you really can. And so the very first thing that we're going to examine, and I'm going to give you kind of a shortcut for the offer process is the prospect. The second thing after we've examined the prospect is we then examine competitors. Competitors here, I'm going to give you this global view for a second and then we're going to drill down into how these apply to the examination for offer creation. Excuse me. So for competitors, right, we need to understand what are they saying to the marketplace? What promises are they making to the, to the marketplace? How are they describing their, their solution? What offers are they presenting to the marketplace? We need to know that if we're going to come up with a superior offer compared to competitors. Well, if we don't know what they're offering, then how do we come up with a superior offer? How would we ever know if our offer was superior? If we don't know what our competitors are offering. And so we've got to, we've got to examine what competitors are saying, how they're describing their product, what promises they're making, what benefits they are. And so we're going to go ahead and convey what the offer is, the price, the terms, the bonuses, the guarantee, all things that we're going to, we're going to talk about in just a little bit. And then of course, right, finally, after we've examined prospects, right? So the, let me, let me say this. The reason why we start with prospects when it comes to examination and then go to competitors is because now we can look at competitors and we can see if certain competitors are off the mark in terms of what it is that they're saying, what it is that they're offering. Based on what we now understand about the prospects, right? So we start with the prospects, the foundation, critical, right? Critical, critical, critical, that we pay attention to prospects or excuse me, examine prospects. Then we go over to competitors. And now we see what our competitors doing, saying all that, which we're going to, we're going to talk about. And then finally, after we examine prospects and competitors, then we go over, that should say, I knew I was going to catch a typo at some point. That should say product. And so prospect competitors, product. And so product, meaning your product, your service. So we only dig into your product, your service to start to identify the benefits, to start to identify the promise, to start to identify what's unique about it, potentially the unique mechanism, right? We only dig into the product, your product, your service. After we've examined prospects and competitors, because if we started with your product or service, or if you started with your product or service, well, then odds are, right, you could see things or believe you're seeing things that could be valuable to the marketplace, but you really don't know what the marketplace wants, right? And so what happens when marketers and entrepreneurs start by examining their own product first, and let me fix this, because this is going to drive me nuts for you. I don't want anybody to be confused. Let me see if I can do this here. Product. That was just driving me nuts. Right, what they, they end up making assumptions about, right, what the market wants, what the market would be excited about, what the market would like, and they're really pulling it from their own internal world or their own internal perspective. You've got to recognize that as an offer creator, as an offer creator first, marketer second, that you've got to learn to see the world through your prospects eyes. It's not about what motivates you. It's not about what excites you. It's not about what drives you. It's all about the market. You've got to learn to step out of your shoes and see the world through their lens, through their eyes. Doesn't matter if there are, there may be lots of things that they get excited about that you wouldn't, right? Doesn't matter, right? Like, it doesn't matter if you wouldn't. It only matters if they would. There are a lot of things that you might get excited about that they wouldn't. Again, like, right? It doesn't matter what you would get excited about. It doesn't matter what you wouldn't get excited about. It doesn't matter what you'd find compelling or what you don't find compelling. It doesn't matter what you'd be interested in. It doesn't matter what you would respond to. It matters what the market is interested in, what the market cares about, what the market feels. market feels and oftentimes for many of us, especially those of us that have been in our market for any significant length of time, you've got to really resist the, you've got to really resist the curse of knowledge, right? Like you've got to resist the curse of knowledge and you got to recognize that more often than not you are not the prospect. You are not your prospect, right? And so if I was marketing, marketing and presenting an offer to a new online entrepreneur, using direct response, I've got to recognize that my perspective, my language, my phraseology, my understanding, my interests, my feelings, my concerns, the things that I might be overwhelmed with, the things that I find easy have nothing whatsoever to do with how the audience sees it. And the only thing that I care about, the only thing that you need to care about is the prospect. Now, we're going to talk about for offer creation to keep this simple. We're going to talk about prospect and competitors. And so let's talk about examining prospects. This is really just a, a questioning kind of flow, a questioning process. This is kind of the way that I would say this is these are things that you need to get clarity on. So first and foremost, right? What problem do your prospects want resolved? What problem do they desperately want resolved that you are resolving? So I don't mean what are all the problems that they have, right, in the world, right? Because I don't think you have a product to take care of all those. But what is the, what is the problem that they want resolved that you are addressing? Now I just want to, I want to explain something very quickly. And this is important that when I say problem in this context, there are really two categories of problems that we deal with in direct response, in marketing with offer creation. One is what most people think of when they think of problem, which is the existence of a pain point, like a frustration, a disappointment, God forbid somebody's got arthritis in their knee and they, they're in pain and they want to get out of pain or God forbid their house is being foreclosed on and they don't know what to do to save it, right? Or, you know, they are, their cardiovascular health is, health is terrible and they want to fix it. And they're scared to death of, you know, having a heart attack, something like that, right? And so that is the existence of a pain point that they want alleviated. But then there's another category of what we call problem in a direct response context. And that is an unfulfilled desire. And so unfulfilled desires are often different from the first category in that sometimes they're not, they're not associated with the presence of a pain point. So the example that I like to use all the time, why I have no idea, but I use this example all the time of, let's say I wanted a green lush lawn, like, you know, move into a new place, want a green lush lawn, not sure what the heck to do. Don't even know what kind of grass is out there. Not a handy dude, like blah, blah, blah, right? But I really would love to have a green lush lawn. It's not that the lawn is dead or it's, you know, it's an eyesore. It's an embarrassment in front of my neighbors. It's just, I really want it to be that much nicer. That's what we would call an unfulfilled desire, right? And so, you know, somebody that, let's say, is in physical shape and maybe wants a six-pack, you know, they want abs. It's not necessarily the presence of a pain point. Somebody that doesn't have abs, they could have emotional turmoil, you know, experience emotional turmoil over it. But that's probably a different issue that needs to be resolved, right? But it's not the presence of a pain point or discomfort. It's just the lack of a desire. It's that I desire something and I don't have it. The reason why I'm explaining that to you is because when I say what problem do they desperately want resolved? I'm using, we're using words like problem and desperately and resolved. I'm talking about, right? It can fall into either of those. So you might be offering, your offer might be designed for an unfulfilled desire or it might be designed for the alleviation of pain. I don't want you to think that, well, I'm not, I'm not focusing on a pain point. I'm focusing on an unfulfilled desire. Therefore, it's not a problem that I'm solving. You are solving a problem, right? You're solving a problem. In the example of, right, like I want a six-pack, right? Like you could make the case that I want a six-pack. I don't have a six-pack, right? In of itself is it's an unfulfilled desire. It's a problem. And maybe I don't even know how to get a six-pack. I've been trying and I don't have one yet. Or, right, I want a green-lush lawn and I don't have one right now. Problem, right? And I don't know how to get one. I don't even know what I should do. That is what we call in a direct response context, the presence of a problem. And so what problem are you solving? Because remember when I said earlier, right, that people buy products and services for the benefits, for the result, excuse me, for the transformation, for the outcome. We could really boil that down to saying that people buy products and services. They spend money on things to resolve problems, to resolve a problem, right? Like my portfolio isn't where I want it to be. That's why I'm investing in this newsletter, because I believe that it will get me to where I want to be. It'll show me how to do it, right? And so people buy products and services to resolve problems. What is the problem, whether it's the pain point or unfulfilled desire, that that they have that you are ultimately going to resolve? You have to be crystal clear on this. You have to be crystal clear on this because the conveying to them and listen to me, let me actually do this for one second. That it's this important. That the I want you to understand this that at the root of what it is that we're doing with an offer, with marketing. Is we're showing the prospect. Listen to me. We're showing the prospect that we've got a different way for them to solve their problem, right? Like that we can solve their problem that if they take advantage of this offer, right? Which ultimately, right, is low risk is all these things that we're going to talk about. That if they take advantage of this offer, they will go from where they are right now to where they want to be. Period. That's ultimately what we are doing. That is the most important part, if you will, the essence of an effective marketing campaign and offer. That's why people respond because I'm here and I want to be here. Can you get me from here to here and get me from here to here better, more officially, more effectively, faster, easier, quicker, whatever than anybody else with less risk? That's ultimately what people want. And so that's ultimately what it is that we are conveying. Everything that we do and say and show and present and demonstrate from in the campaign all the way through to the offer is there to show the prospect. Yes, if you respond to this, you can go from where you are right now to where you want to be. You need to know where are they right now that they don't want to be and where do they want to be? This is why you do this. The examination. This is why you have to answer the question of what is the problem that they have that they desperately want solved that you solve? And so let's go back here. Let's go back here. And so hopefully you guys can see my screen again. Somebody let me know you can see the slide deck. Good. Cool. Yes, perfect. OK. So. What problem do they desperately want resolved? Next is what do they want in a solution? And so what do I mean by? Thank you guys, Leslie, young Mao, if I'm pronouncing that correctly, I'm terrible with names, Brett, Chris. You guys are amazing. Thank you. Thank you, Sarkis. Cool. OK, awesome. So what do they want in a solution? Like, right? What is an ideal solution for the prospect? Like, what are one of the ways that you can start to develop this with by asking kind of both of these questions? Like, what do they want in a solution? And what don't they want in a solution? Right? Like, in other words, like we could certainly, you know, like, let's think about something like just, for example. Yeah, like, right? I mean, said, you know, solution with less efforts, like, right? Like, you could start to think about, let's say, let's say we were talking about SEO and getting SEO rankings. Well, the reality is, is that, like, right? How how attractive would our solution be if there was a massive amount of manual labor that had to be done where they had to work with a spreadsheet and go out and find websites, search, search on Google, go out, find them, search in the different websites and then, you know, paste the link back in the spreadsheet. Right. So we we could start to say, well, they don't want, like, when it comes to getting SEO rankings, like, most people aren't going to find it wildly attractive to do a whole bunch of manual effort. Right. Like, right. And so we can start to list that under what don't they want. And right. And and from that, we can get the reverse for what they do want. Right. So when we identify what they don't want, we can see the flip side of that of ultimately what they do want. But this is where you really want to have an understanding of really the way that the way to really boil this down for, you know, YWO language is what solution. What type of solution would your prospects find attractive and what what type of solution would your prospects find unattractive. Right. What type of solutions. Right. And what's the what what different aspects of a solution would they find attractive, compelling, like cool. And what what aspects or attributes of a solution would they find unattractive and and tedious and, you know, something that they don't want. And so what type of solution are they are they looking for. Another part of this and this is really important is what are the solutions that are already being presented to them. And if anything, they're being hammered. Right. Like, you know, like, let's say, for example, in the copy world writing copy, for example. Well, I love talking about copy. I love writing copy. It was one of the very first things that I fell in love with 20 plus years ago. And and I love it. Right. And I have a copywriting training that I think is the best on the planet. I'm biased, of course. But but the reality is, is that right? There are tons of solutions and have been up to fairly recently for like learn how to write copy. And it was videos or a lot, you know, videos and and and fill in the blanks and blah, blah, blah. And right. Those are a dime a dozen today. Unfortunately. Right. Those are a dime a dozen today. I wish they weren't, but those are a dime a dozen today. And so when I'm looking at what what kind of solution do they want? Part of this is right. What have they already been hammered over the head with a dozen times? What would be a variation like that they write? You might you might describe something that is not even in existence yet in terms of they would love hand holding like really, right? Like part of this offer for YWO was I was like, you know what? I know that this is the most valuable thing. And I just want to I want to pay my team to get on one on one with everybody that is willing to go through this. This train, invest the time, energy, money, effort to go through this with me. I want to pay my team to get one on one with people to actually do the offer with them live so they can see it. And I was like, I like that a lot because nobody else is offering that. And this is a critical component. And so when I looked at the solution, like part of what what what do they want? What do people want? What what don't what don't people want? Part of that was seeing what they were what the marketplace, what our tribe is already being hammered with from others. And so hopefully that makes sense to you guys. If not, we can certainly talk about that during the Q&A. This what other solutions have prospects been exposed to? Which ones are most popular? Which ones are most popular? Which ones are in high demand like types of solutions? Because you can see for what it's worth, markets evolve. I want you to pay attention to me for for a second. What we're going to do is probably in another 12 minutes or so, we'll take our first bathroom break. I can't believe it. And so but I want you to pay attention to me here. And so, you know, listen to me, markets evolve over time. And so just because, let's say, just to give an example, just because, you know, coaching programs were wildly popular in one marketplace, you know, in a marketplace, let's say a year ago, that doesn't mean that a year from now coaching programs are going to still be wildly popular, right? Because markets evolve, markets evolve in terms of sophistication, in terms of what they've been exposed to, in terms of what it is that they value, in terms of looking for the next quote unquote innovation or disruption. And so you've got to look and you've got to see what are the solutions that what are the solutions that the marketplace has already been exposed to, right? And ultimately what we're looking for here, and you're going to see how we use this all later, so don't worry, right? We're ultimately we're looking for the opportunity to engineer something better, to engineer something superior, to build in. I don't mean engineer, like create a whole new product. That's not what I'm talking about. I'm talking about engineering a better superior offer. And so, right, what are the solutions that they've been exposed to? What are the solutions have they been hammered with? What are the solutions are now considered or viewed as kind of dated and right? What are the solutions that aren't dated yet and are kind of popular? Right? Sometimes this is different models to, let's say, in the in the information world or the coaching world to the way the coaching is being delivered, right? So you could see even in the evolution of markets. A while ago, there were online courses and that was it. It was just like online courses. There were even prior to courses that were just like books, e-books. When I first got started, it was like download a digital e-book, right? And then it became like, you know, multiple collection of e-books. And then when video, like, right, I was around when video just came online. Funniest thing I tell this all the time as a side note, I had to pay a thousand bucks to learn how to put video online. Seriously, well, years ago, this was, you know, like pre-YouTube, you know, this was there was no word, there was no click, none of that kind of stuff. Nuts. Anyway, right. And then, of course, right, then there became courses. Then there became, you know, courses plus with coaching, you know, programs. Then there became right with like coaching where it was like a live Q&A. And then it kind of evolved to this cohort model where now you're going to go through it live and right, things continue to evolve as markets become more sophisticated. So we're looking to see is our solution that the way that we're positioning it, the way that we're thinking of framing it, is it already being kind of hammered to the marketplace? Is there already being, you know, are they already being exposed to it constantly? Next, how does the prospect feel about the problem? Like, so what are the emotions that they have about the problem? Right? Like, is it frustration? Is it, you know, anguish? Is it fear? Is it disgust? Is it anger? Right? Is it excitement? Like, in terms of the desire, like, is it, you know, coming from this place of motivational joy, if you will, how does the prospect feel about the problem? This is important. You're going to see how this is important when it comes to the presentation of benefits during the proposition portion of the offer. And so, right, how do they feel about the problem? And how do they feel about the current available solutions? In other words, right? Like, so if overwhelmed and defeated, right? April, that's certainly like, you know, and this is important. You know, one other thing that I'll say is when we're doing this, like, you know, especially when it comes to emotions, this is a question that I get asked all the time from our E5 clients and students, like, when we're talking about emotions and they're like, how do I know? Like, is it, you know, frustration like or is it like, you know, disappointment? And the answer is, is that it's not that black and white, right? Like, we're not that we're not black and white creatures, right? We're very complex, dynamic creatures. There are a lot of lines. There's a lot of gray, right? There's a lot of gray. It's not a matter of identifying the right word to ultimately describe what it is that they're feeling. It's to make sure that you have clarity and a grasp of the sentiment that your prospects have, like, right? Some people might be coming into your campaign and for some of you, excited, motivated, you know, jazz, like pumped, eager, greedy, like that sort of thing. Other people might be coming in, you know, nervous, scared, anxious. All of those are kind of a blend, right? So it's not, well, is it nervous or is it anxious? Like, don't even sweat that. It's this, it's this, it's this vibe, the general vibe that you want to you want to get. And so what's the general vibe in terms of how how your prospects feel about their problem and what's the general vibe of how they feel about the current available solutions, the thing, the options that they currently have? Are they disgusted? Are they right? Are they skeptical? Are they jaded or are they jazzed about it? Do they view it as, you know, some of these things as a new breakthrough? Right. And this is important because, right, if you look at things like, let's say, in the investing world, if you look at things like, let's say, crypto versus, versus, let's say, you know, index funds or something like that, right? Index funds being an old school, you know, very ultra conservative investment vehicle and crypto being the antithesis of that, right? Well, there's a different vibe, a different feeling in terms of emotions and views and viewpoint when it comes to people that are interested in crypto compared to the people that are interested in index funds, right? So while some of the people that are interested in crypto are wildly excited, you know, just oozing with greed or some of those people might also be wildly terrified if they're already in crypto. When it comes to index funds, you're not going to have the people that are interested there, you're not going to have those extreme emotions just by the nature of the solution. And so you really want to just get a handle on on what is the general sentiment that your market has about the problem and about the available solutions. And then what beliefs? Now, these are just general beliefs. Do they have which you need to understand to best resonate and align with them? So this doesn't apply to every single one of you. But the example that I like to always use is so one of my clients is this behemoth newsletter company, one point five billion dollar a year company. And they the majority of their subscribers are like, you know, 50 plus white men, you know, right wing, you know, conservative guys, you know, right wrong or indifferent. And so, you know, obviously, we know that there are certain beliefs that they hold, right? Certain beliefs that they hold about government, about about the current president, about, you know, Congress, about the Clintons, right? Like about, you know, former President Obama, like, right? There are certain beliefs that they have, you know, like that the Democrats are evil and, you know, and Trump is, you know, the Messiah or whatever. And I'm just goofing, even though many people do think that. What was I going to say? I just lost my train of thought. Oh, and so the reason why that's valuable, the reason why that's important to understand is because so this particular client, the agoracompany, so like they know that if they're ever going to mention or use, let's say, and again, right, Roman, this is not about politics. It's about understanding your audience and understanding the beliefs that they have and why it's important and how we use it. Right. Like if they were going to talk about the Clintons, they've got to talk about the Clintons in a negative light in their marketing. If they don't talk about the Clintons in a negative light and they chose to talk about them in a positive light, like let's say they had a campaign that was like, you know, the smartest investment vehicle discovered by Hillary Clinton, how you can use it to to become wealthy in the next 18 months or something like that. The market would absolutely not respond to them. Right. Why? Because that would not resonate with their belief. That would butt up against that would contradict what it is that that marketplace believes, not every marketplace has that same belief, but it's important to just understand are there certain beliefs that they have that that we need to know? So that number one, we don't contradict them. Number two, that we can use them to our advantage when talking about the the offer. Same thing applies to right, wrong or indifferent. Most people don't view attorneys favorably. You know, they don't they don't view attorneys favorably. Most many people, especially in the deeper alternative health space, really have disdain for the pharmaceutical companies. Right. And so like pharmaceutical companies are out to, you know, to keep you hooked on medication, to keep these phenomenal natural treatments from you. And so see, that's important to understand. It's important to understand, right? Because again, you know, a novice marketer could make the mistake of thinking like, right, like, you know, pharmaceutical, you know, pharmaceutical approved, you know, natural, nutrient, you know, great to fight off, you know, cancer, that's, you know, that is a big mistake. Merely for one reason and one reason only, because it does not resonate with an existing belief. And so you've got to you absolutely have to understand. Are there any existing beliefs that the market has that we need to that we need to know or that that we could use to resonate with them or that we need to make sure we don't contradict? So now let's talk about examining competitors, which is significantly easier. And it's kind of really just in generally, it is really just seeing what it is that competitors are saying related to what it is that we just uncovered with prospects. So for example, what problem are they solving? Right. So what's the problem like? And this is where we can we can learn from savvy competitors. So number one, let me say this to be clear. I'm not I'm not a believer in in funnel hacking. I love Russell. He's a good friend. I'm going out to Boise soon to speak for him at something. Great marketer, incredible businessman, love click funnels. But I'm not a believer in in funnel hacking. When I talk about examining competitors, most of what it is that we're doing, most of what it is that we're looking at is to see what competitors are saying. So we know what not to say. Right. So let me say that again. Most of what it is that most of why we're doing what we're doing is not to funnel hack or copy or model. It is to see what competitors are saying. So we know what not to say. And what I mean by that is not because we're making a judgment that competitors are wrong, but because differentiation is critical in marketing, marketing more so again today than ever before is the art of meaningful differentiation. Marketing is the art of meaningful differentiation, meaningfully differentiating your product, ultimately your offer from competitors. And so I'm not looking at competitors to understand what to say to see what they're saying, so I can say the same thing. I'm looking what they're saying so that I don't double up and model what it is that they're saying. But in this context, right, what problem are our competitors solving? This could be insightful for us because if we see a trend across a number of competitors, right, well, there might be something for us to learn from that. If we see multiple competitors that are all addressing the same problem and their direct response marketers and they've been around for any significant length of time, then there's a good chance that they've identified the core problem that the market has. The other reason why we ask this question is because this may show you that there is a problem that is going unaddressed in the marketplace that you see and know from your examination is a critical problem to the marketplace. And so it gives us the opportunity to go in and and address that problem. So what problem are competitors solving? What promise are they making? So I didn't get a chance earlier to talk about this. This is usually something that, again, this is more marketing campaign-ish, but it does carry over to the offer, which is why I'm sharing it in here. And that is you got to understand that, right? That ultimately what creates engagement with your marketing campaign, what gets people excited and what ultimately gets them interested in the offer is the promise that you're making. And so, you know, like the promise, what is the promise of the offer? What's the promise of the product? And when we say promise, I mean, ultimately, what kind of outcome, transformation results, are you promising them that they will experience? What's the change that you'll promise them they will experience? What's the pain that you're promising them they'll get out of? And how quickly and when? Right. And what do they need to do? It could be right. And so also, what's the, you know, what unfulfilled desire? Are you promising them that they're going to experience and how and when and where? And so what is the promise that they are making? The promise is at the foundation of marketing, right? Marketing is ultimately, if you really think about it, you know, I don't want to be careful here, but marketing is ultimately just a it is one gigantic promise with proof is really what it is. It's like one gigantic promise with proof, excuse me. And then an offer to experience the promise that's really ultimately what it is. Right. Like. What I'm about to show you is how you can experience XYZ, how you can get that six pack of abs in the next, you know, in the next three weeks without having to die, without having to do exercise, without having to take any crazy pills, do anything at night. In fact, what you're about to see is how to strip off 15 pounds of fat from your waist, you know, in 60 seconds or less unheard of. I know I'm going to show you all the proof, show you the new discovery, but right. And so ultimately, it's one gigantic promise with proof and then an offer to experience the promise. And so. And so what promise are they making? So right, like, what is the promise that they are saying to two prospects that prospects will experience when they when they take advantage of their offer, when they take advantage, when they get their product or their service? What are they promising prospects they will experience? This is really important stuff to to understand. Number one, this gives us the ability to see are there any trends that we need to pay attention to? Like, is there a particular outcome that the majority of competitors are promising? Right. Is there is there like there's a lot and we're going to circle back to this. But what promise are they are they making? What are the attributes of their solution? You're going to understand more about attributes of a solution in a little bit when we get to the panacea portion of the winning offer. Attributes like consist of, you know, how much work is involved? How much energy does it take? How much of a learning curve is there? How uncomfortable is it to use the the the solution? How much risk is associated with the solution? How safe is the solution? How consistently does it work? Right. Those are all attributes of the solution. And so is it, you know, are they talking about how fast it works? Are they talking about how easy it is? Are they talking about how simple it is to to get started? Are they talking about how safe it is? How little risk there is with it? Are they talking about a combination of those things? What are they talking about? What are the attributes that they are claiming for their solution? This is valuable stuff for us, because again, it shows us how to differentiate. Right. It shows us how to position our product against competitors. Just remember, like Sarkis, you know, posted with all caps in the in the chat. Again, never forget at the root, the foundation. Marketing is the art of meaningful differentiation above all else, especially. And I don't want to go down this path because I can get this was this is a this was a one day training in and in and of itself on this topic. That the reality is, is that, right, people aren't interested in more of the same. People want different. They want to know, like, right, they want to know what's different about yours. What's different about the way that your product or service works? What's different about what you're claiming about your product or your your service, about what it does, about how it does it, about how I can get it and benefit from it, about how quick it'll work, about how easy it is, about how safe it is. Ultimately, differentiation is critical because rarely do people spend money on more of the more of the same. Rarely really, the reality is, is that rarely do people engage with marketing and messages that are nearly more about the same. And so again, what we're doing here is gathering fodder and insight to use when we put together our our offer. What is their unique mechanism? So the unique mechanism, if any, so I want to because I don't want to get into the weeds here with this, but this is valuable and important. And you are going to experience this on your on your creation session. Is the unique mechanism is really simple. The unique mechanism is how your product or service works to deliver the result. Right. So it is right. And the reality is, is that every product or service has a mechanism. In other words, every product or service that works to produce a change or result and outcome has a mechanism. The way to think about a mechanism is it's how your product or service works. So if you said, right, if I ask you, well, what's the promise promises? This take the supplement and it'll bring down your blood pressure. And then I asked you, well, how does it work to bring down blood pressure? Like, why is it able to work to bring down blood pressure? How does it work? And you would be able to explain to me, well, it's X, Y, Z nutrients in there. And what they do is they expand the arteries, they loosen the arteries, and therefore they bring down the, you know, blah, blah, blah. They make the arteries more pliable because blah, blah, blah. Right. And so you'd be able to explain that that's a mechanism. Right. And so the key is, right, is identifying how your product or service works to deliver the result and how it works to deliver the result differently from the majority of competitors out there. And I don't want to get into the weeds here. I don't want to confuse anybody here because this is more of a marketing tactic than it is anything, but we do use it in the, in the offer when possible. But what you're looking for is are your competitors claiming that they have a unique mechanism? Is there a unique system, a unique framework, a unique algorithm, a unique formula, a unique process that they have named that they are claiming is the reason why their product or service is able to deliver the result better than anybody else. And some of the other, some of the folks that have been through E5 on here. And I'm not even sure who's on live. I know a lot of people are going to be watching the recording. But if you're on here live and you've been through E5 and somebody has a question about the unique mechanism fire away in the chat and those folks will be able to help you. But it's again, it is how your product or service works to deliver the promise, how it works to deliver the result. And in a unique way compared to competitors. That's all we're talking about. So again, every product has a mechanism because every product that works, there's a reason why it works. That reason why it works is what we would refer to as the mechanism, whether it's a unique mechanism or not, is all depending on on whether other competitors, their products work the same way and whether anybody has named it and is showing what makes it different in the way that it works. And so are they claiming that there's any, again, unique framework, process, system, algorithm, whatever, behind how their product is able to deliver such great results. And then ultimately, what is their proposition and the proposition we are going to cover in just a little bit. The proposition portion of a winning offer is what most people think of as an offer. And so, right, you've heard me say in the in the messaging for this, and we're going to take our first bathroom, our first bio break in in just two minutes. You heard me say that the difference between the traditional offer and a winning offer is a winning offer is built with three legs, three kind of components, and the regular offer is really one component. That one component that makes up a regular offer is a component is one of the three for a winning offer. And that's typically what we refer to as the proposition. The proposition is what, you know, when you think of an offer, you think of, well, what are the price? What do I get? Right? What are the deliverables? What's the price? What's the terms? What's the bonuses? What's the risk reversal? That is the proposition. And so ultimately here, and this is one of the most important steps when we're examining competitors, we want to know what is their proposition? So what are they offering? What are the deliverables? What do I get if I respond to competitor A? What do I actually get in terms of the deliverables? Do I get videos? I get audios. I get this. I get this. We need to know that. Right? What is the what? What are the what? What's the price? What's the terms which we're going to cover? So you're going to understand all this by the time we're done. What are the terms? What are the the I said, bonuses, price, terms, and what what's the risk reversal proposition, all of which we're going to we're going to cover? You want to know that. The reason why we need to know this, you need to know this, you want to know this is because when we get into the proposition, we're ultimately going to learn this in just a little bit. When we get into the proposition, what we are creating or what we're engineering or attempting to engineer is what's called a sin proposition. Sin stands for superior, irresistible, no brainer. Superior is about competitors. Superior, right? Superior to what? How do we say your offer is superior? Superior to what? Superior to competitors. How do you know if your offer is superior to competitors? If you don't know what competitors are are offering, if you don't know their proposition. So in order for us to be able to put together a superior proposition, we need to know what your competitors are are proposing or are offering. And so only through that, it's very similar to, by the way, when it comes to even the unique mechanism, how do you know you have a unique mechanism if you don't examine competitors to identify if they're claiming they have a unique mechanism? Right? Because maybe you think you have a unique mechanism, but because you never examine competitors, you really don't. You have the same mechanism as two of them. Right? And now you don't have a unique mechanism. You just have a mechanism. The only way for you to know with certainty that you have a unique mechanism or that you have a superior proposition is by starting with the examination. And so this is why we start with the examination. I'm going to say this before we take our first bio break that, you know, the examination stage, the examination process for me is the worst. Right? Like I don't, you know, I don't enjoy it. It's not fun. Right? It's not that creative, right? Brain activity. It's certainly not planning and plotting and and and engineering and offer and then creating, you know, developing, you know, a big idea and then writing the lead and the marketing argument, all things, you know, talk about any five. But it's where the money is made. It's where the money is made. I could show you streams of quotes from some of the best copywriters and marketers in the world talking about the value of research from some of the old school copywriters like Gene Schwartz, Breakthrough Advertising, John Capels, my buddy Mark Ford, Brian Kurtz from from boardroom, Clayton Makepeace. I could go on and on about the value of research. It is where all the gold is found, right? Because it's how you the better you keep your finger on the pulse of your market, your prospects, your competitors, the better your offers will be, the better your marketing will be, the more your messaging and your offers will resonate with people, the more you know about them, the easier the game becomes, period. Just make sure we're all good. Yes, perfect. Okay, awesome. Chris, Leslie, cool. So let's talk about David. Thank you so much, Brett. Yeah, awesome. You guys are amazing. Thank you so much. Thank you for the engagement. I love it. Makes me feel great to know that you guys are engaged in paying attention. So let's talk about the components of a winning offer. This is very simple and very straightforward. I told you that there are three legs to a winning offer. There are three kind of aspects to a winning offer. The first one is what I call an attractive panacea. It's really an attractive solution. I use panacea because I wanted three Ps, to be honest with you. And so it's really an attractive solution is what we're talking about. Next is a sin proposition. And then finally, and this is the part that a lot of people neglect is the presentation, which is the flow and the language in use, which is so important. And so it's these three, this combination of these three components that make up a winning offer. When we have an attractive, panacea, an attractive solution, which we're going to talk about, and part of that is also marketing, positioning, framing. When we have a sin proposition, and when we present it with the right flow and the right languaging, you have the makings for an incredible offer. And so what we're going to do is we're going to start and we're going to go through these three. Now you can see in the upper left hand corner, you can see this is the first of three. So as we progress, just so that you know where you are, you'll see us progress through these three, just so that you can, again, you can know exactly where you are. Now I want to start with this as it relates to an attractive solution and attractive panacea. And that is this, that what I want you to understand, I want you to get this, I want you to pay attention to this, because this is, it took me a very long time to realize this, that there are a lot of people out there that talk about this idea of an irresistible offer. And right, and it's almost become cliche, this irresistible offer. The reality is, right, like if you really think about it, the majority of marketers that are at least somewhat experienced, they've heard irresistible offer out the wazoo at this point. The reality is, is that most of their offers are wildly resistible, right? They're wildly resistible. They're the farthest, even though they know irresistible and they've heard it and they get it and they understand it, right, and whatnot, most of the offers out there are wildly resistible. And so what most people think of immediately, and you're going to see how this goes back to what it is that we talked about earlier, what most people think of immediately when they think of making or engineering an irresistible offer is where do they go? They go to the proposition. They go to what, what are the, what does the prospect get, like the deliverables? What is the price? What are the terms? What are the bonuses? And what's the risk reversal? They immediately go to the proposition elements, right? And those proposition elements are the same proposition elements in an average, in an average offer and in a winning offer. It's how we do them and how we execute on them that makes the difference. But that's where most people go is they think about the proposition and what they neglect is they neglect the, they neglect that, they neglect to address what hopefully you guys and gals now understand when we talked earlier about what does the prospect get and what does the prospect need to do to get it? And we talked about in there more than just, right, the typical irresistible offer. Okay, go to proposition, right? Because what does the prospect get? Let me give them a bunch of stuff. Let me load it up with a bunch of stuff. And what do they need to do to get it? Let me kind of make it a cheap price, right? Fill out the order form and they're off to the raises. See how that all ties together, that whole mindset of what is right? What's an offer? It's what the prospect gets and what they need to do to get it. What does that mean that what we're talking about is what do I actually give them, right? Like the deliverables and what do they need to do to get it? They need to pay and they need to go to an order form and real simple order form. And so that's where they operate. And what happens from that mindset is they end up neglecting, they end up neglecting one of the foundational keys when they don't realize that what the prospect gets is more than just the deliverables, it's the benefits, the results, it's the the risk reversal and the confidence and whatnot. And they certainly neglect to really understand the impact of what does the prospect need to do to get it? The things like time, commitment, effort, discomfort, work, money, risk, all that kind of stuff. And what ends up happening is what most people do to really just kind of boil it down is they end up offering a really ugly solution with a what they believe is an irresistible proposition. So they've taken an unattractive solution, a solution that the marketplace doesn't find wildly attractive, and they've tried to wrap it in an irresistible proposition. And the example that I'd like to give you is this, I don't know why I going back to this whole green lawn thing, I got to come up with a better example, but this should hopefully this is an extreme example and this will make sense. And so like, look, I could come across somebody that has a and I don't my lawn is fine, by the way, I might come across if I was in that market for that type of help and guidance, I had that kind of unfulfilled desire, I could come across an offer that was like pay nothing today was a soft offer, pay nothing today, right, use the you know, use the solution for the next whatever 30 days. And, and I either have a green lawn or I don't have to pay anything. And I get these cool bonuses, these guides and whatnot, to go with it and these cheat sheets and everything. And then, you know, six months later, if my lawn still isn't gorgeous and green, I can get all my money back plus, you know, an extra 50 bucks or gift card or whatever, right. And on the surface, that seems like a wildly irresistible proposition, it seems like on the surface that that is a proposition that is difficult to say no to. But right, if the solution involved or required me sleeping outside for 48 hours on my lawn and every hour on the hour having to dig up my lawn expose the soil with my bare hands and then spit some saliva in it and put the dirt back and do that every hour on the hour for 48 hours straight, I don't care how irresistible the proposition is, I ain't buying, right. I ain't buying. And so don't like, I don't even want it for free because I'm not interested because I'm not willing to do that because to me, the value exchange of what it is that I have to do to get the value, the benefit, the payoff is so ridiculous, is so absurd, is so out of the realm, right. And so my point in sharing that with you is that, right, an irresistible proposition for an unattractive solution still will not sell, still will not sell. And so you've got to begin first before you form the proposition component, right, ultimately we want to start with a wildly attractive solution. Now, right, we want to start with a wildly attractive solution first, we want to start with a wildly attractive solution, and then we want to be able to offer it with a sin, superior irresistible, no-brainer proposition, and then present it with the right flow and language. But the very first thing, right, is the quality and attractiveness of the solution that you're offering. This is ultimately the thing that they're going to do, what has to actually be done, right, for them to get the reward. It's just like even with like consulting, where if you had an agency, but yet part of what it is that you did was they had to show up every day for an hour at 7am in the morning, right, for the next three weeks. Well, I don't really care what your proposition is, I don't care how much cheaper it is than everybody else, I don't care what bonuses you're going to give me, I don't care what risk reversal you're going to offer me, I'm not interested in doing that. That is so unattractive, it's so unappealing, right, that it doesn't matter what the proposition is. And so we always begin with the most attractive solution that we possibly can. Now, this part of the winning offer creation process or engineering process is probably the one that is most limited, because what this really ultimately involves is your product, is ultimately involves your solution. And so this one is arguably the most difficult to really move, but not impossible by any stretch and not hard, it's the most difficult of everything, of the three, but not hard. And so what I want to cover with you is what is viewed generally as an attractive panacea, an attractive solution. And so this is critical. So number one of an attractive panacea solution is of course, at the foundation that it solves a significant problem or unfulfilled desire for the prospect, right. So once again, let's be crystal clear, and I don't mean to continue to kind of cover these foundational things, but I want to just reinforce these critical points that if your offer, if your solution doesn't solve a problem, a pressing problem that your prospect has or unfulfilled desire, nothing else matters, because what people buy is the resolution of the problem. And so, right, like somebody could offer a great solution for something, right, with an irresistible proposition, right. And if I'm not interested or I don't see that I have that problem or believe that I have that problem, then I'm not buying it, right. I'm not buying it. And so, right, it solves a significant problem or unfulfilled desire. Now, on that kind of flip side, which we already talked about, it also produces a big desirable result. And so this is key because I want you to understand something here, because this goes back to the saying of, right, is the juice worth the squeeze. So hypothetically, right, you might, let's say, let's say you're selling a course on your program or a training or something, or agency services or consulting services, whatever, or software, on getting more traffic, right. And so what you're addressing is the lack of traffic for a particular market. And you ultimately are able to promise them that they will get more traffic, right, if they do this. These first two bullets of an attractive solution. Well, if your promise is that with this solution, you will get a whopping eight additional visitors per week to your website. Odds are for the majority of you, that ain't going to be a big desirable result. The juice isn't worth the squeeze. In other words, for eight additional visitors to our website per week, unless you run a very high ticket, ultra targeted concierge type of service, or you have an ultra high ticket, right, and there's a small marketplace, and right, which is the majority of you don't fall into that category, that's not a big desirable result. And so ultimately, what you are looking for here is you're looking for a solution, or you're looking to engineer and create a solution that is able to produce a big desirable result. Now I want to be crystal clear about something here. I am not saying merely promise a big desirable result. That's not what I'm saying, right. I'm not saying lie. I'm not saying, well, if your thing only can can can produce eight additional visitors a week and just promise them 8000 a week. That's not at all what I'm saying. That's why I said to you guys before we dove into this slide, that this is about engineering. This is about tweaking, changing, optimizing, improving. This is about taking a good hard look at your solution, really asking yourself is what this thing produces in its current form, right, enough juice to warrant the squeeze, right, like enough of a result, enough of a payoff to warrant responding to the offer and implementing the solution. And so you really want a big desirable result. This is also why as a side note, why it's so valuable and so necessary to keep your finger on the pulse of your market, why it's so valuable and necessary for you to do the examination. Because if you don't do the examination and you don't keep your finger on the pulse of the market and you don't know what the market has heard already seen already what the market is is what the market considers like heard it seen it been there done it. Well, then how do you ultimately know what the market is going to see as a big desirable result, big desirable result? Remember, this is not what you feel is a big desirable result. It's what the market feels is a big desirable result. And so ultimately, we want a solution, right, that solves a problem or unfulfilled desire that the marketplace has that they already know they have and that they want resolved. And by solving it, it produces a big desirable result, a big desirable outcome for them. Right. And so somebody is interested in like, you know, losing weight. The reality is right, if you said your diet, you know, your, your, your diet program or your fitness methodology is going to help people lose a quarter pound a week. Right. The majority of people might not be interested in that. Why? Because again, the juice is not worth the squeeze. And so you've got to recognize that. Right. This is why we often say, and I've been saying this for years, right, that you want a big, bold, audacious promise that is both true and believable. Right. And so a big, bold, audacious promise, I can't use any more adjectives in their big, bold, audacious promise. Right. That is true and believable. And so ultimately, you've got to take a good, hard look at your solution and you've got to really ask yourself, like, man, is this producing a big desirable result based on what the marketplace would feel. Next is, is it viewed as quick and easy to implement? Right. I could really rephrase this a number of ways. And I actually did rephrase this bullet a number of ways. So right, this is, right, is there an opportunity for us to position it as quick and easy to implement? And so you're going to understand more about that in a little bit, because there are different ways without lying. There are different ways to talk about certain results rather than other results to demonstrate, you know, quick or easy or, or fast or speed, right, is viewed as producing fast results. Right. And so ultimately, right, like what we're talking about here is it's easy and the results are fast, right, the faster and easier the results, right, the faster the results and the easier it is to get the results, the more valuable your solution is. I think for those of you that read my buddy Alex Hormozzi's book, I think that he did an excellent job on this area, right. I think that in terms of in this area, he did an amazing job. I think it's a great book, regardless, but I think he did an amazing job in this area of really demonstrating the value of, of a, and the, and the kind of the, the correlation between perceived value of a solution and speed and ease. And so, right, the, because you got to understand when we talked about, you know, when we talked about an unattractive solution, well, an unattractive solution is one that is difficult to use, right, and produces slow results, right, difficult and slow, difficult and slow, unattractive, right, easy and fast, attractive, right. And of course, I know that there is a spectrum and it's all relative, right, it's all relative to a marketplace. And so, right, this is not about like, you might think a month is fast, right, but your marketplace might feel like two weeks is slow. And so, right, you've got to really recognize what the marketplace views also what the marketplace has heard and seen before. But so let's just again, recap, because I want you to see this in context, right, that it solves a significant problem run fulfilled desire that your prospects know they have and want resolved. And in the process, it produces a big desirable result. And it produces that big desirable result quickly and easily, right, quickly and easily, it's quick and easy to implement, right, and those results come quick, those the results come quick. Now, one thing that you'll notice here just as a little teaser for what's to come is that let's say it produces a big desirable result of let's go back to weight loss and say that you tell people that if they follow your program, they can lose 50 pounds, right, like this is a program specifically designed for people that want to lose 50 pounds, minimum 50 pounds. And you guarantee that it'll take off 50 pounds. Does that mean that you need to promise them that they'll take off 50 pounds? In a month? Absolutely not. Number one, it would be a lie. Number two, it wouldn't even be believable for most people. Number three, there is an opportunity for us to talk about and we're going to talk about this in just a little bit. But there's an opportunity for us to talk about that it produces fast results, meaning maybe it starts working the very night you jump on the diet, maybe it increases your metabolism and starts burning extra calories, right, burning fat from around your waist the very first day that you begin the diet program. And so right, there is the ability to say that, right, in that context in this little example, that it does produce fast results, right, it begin, in fact, it begins working immediately, the very first night that you go on this program, your body is going to be burning more calories, more fat, you're going to be shedding weight, you might not see it on the in the mirror, right, but over dot over time, right, in just a short period of days, you're going to see X amount of weight, and you're going to continue to see that consistently going forward, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. And so there's still an opportunity to talk about producing fast results without having to talk about the end result, the main end result. And so important for you to understand next, it's viewed as new and different. And so right, like what I'm talking about here, now look, let me say this, is that, is that this is right in an ideal world, we are in an ideal world, our solution would meet all of these criteria, right, that would be like you score a perfect 100. Does that mean that if you only have 90% of these bullets nailed, like when you're done engineering that it's a failure, no, right, understand that this would be like the in the in the perfect world, right, every bullet that I put up here, your solution would meet the criteria. But it doesn't mean that you have to have all these criteria. And so is viewed as new and different. I already said that, you know, like, look, and this is just in general marketing, what people pay attention to is new and different, people want new and different, right, at a bare minimum, I'm going to say different. And so, you know, people aren't interested in more of the same, right, people are not interested in more of the same, right, nobody shows up to a party or a social gathering and says, hey, so what's the same in your life that that was six months ago, nobody's interested in that, they want to know what's new, what's different, what's changed. And so same thing, right, when it comes to information, when it comes to training, when it comes to methods, what people want is new and different. And so if your solution is different from the others, right, then it does a lot of things well beyond the scope of this training, it stimulates hope in your prospects, because it gives them this feeling that this is different than anything I've tried before anything that I've heard before, maybe this is the missing piece that I've been lacking before. And so maybe this is the answer, maybe this is the thing that's finally going to bring me to the to the promised land, so to speak. And so different is wildly valuable in marketing in general, but it's especially valuable in your in your when it comes to your solution. So again, right, and a lot of this is framing, right, a lot of this is positioning, you're going to get to see this done, like you're going to get to see on your creation session, you're going to get to see this done. But it is, you know, it's often, you know, it's often turning the ordinary into the extraordinary by reframing it by repositioning it rather than just accepting that, well, this is a pure commodity. The fact of the matter is for all of you without going down this crazy path, you never want to market and sell a commodity. A commodity in this context is something that people can go and get the same exact thing somewhere else. If you're marketing and selling a commodity, your price is ultimately determined by the marketplace and the accepted price of that thing. And so you don't want to sell a commodity, you want to sell a differentiated solution. Remember what I said earlier, marketing is the art of meaningful differentiation. Part of that is differentiating our solution from every other solution. Ultimately, what you want to be able to say is that I've got a different way to give you the result, the promise that you want. My product, my service works differently to give you that outcome, that transformation, that result from everything else that you've likely tried or heard about up to this point. Right. Ultimately, that's what you want to be able to sell. Those of you that have been through E5 acquisition accelerator, the E5 masterclass, you know that that's the heart and soul of an E5 campaign of an E5 message is demonstrating is ultimately bringing to the market. I've got something different and this different approach, right, is works better at producing the result that you want than anything else. So ultimately, we want our solution to be viewed as new and different in the prospect size. Next is that we want your prospect to see it as superior to other options. Now I want to spend a little bit of time talking about this to just unpack this a little bit. So number one, right, like people want the number one solution. Nobody I've said this many times before and it's partial, you know, partially joking, but it's really not. Nobody goes to Google and searches for the third best way to lose weight. Nobody's interested in the second best way to drive traffic, right? You ain't going to find a whole lot of searches there, right. And that's because people aren't interested in the inferior. People want the superior. People want the best right solution. Now the reality is, is that there are a lot of criteria that you can claim superiority with, right? So yours could be the fastest. It could be the easiest. It could be the safest. It could be the most consistent. It could be the most reliable. There's a lot of those kind of superlatives that you can claim, but we need to be able to ideally, we want to be able to claim superiority in one or more of those criteria for your solution because nobody wants just another solution. Nobody wants, again, nobody wants the second best or third best. They want to know that this is superior, right? And so we're ultimately looking for, is it, it's either the easiest, it's either the fastest, it's either the safest, it's either the most reliable. It either produces the most amount of results, right? So like, look, and I want you to answer, I want you to realize something that the reality is, is that we can have a diet program or a fitness program that is super easy to implement. That doesn't mean that it's going to produce fast results, that doesn't mean that it's gonna produce a lot of results, that doesn't mean it's gonna produce consistent results, right? That doesn't mean it's safe. Excuse me, or safest. We could have something that produces fast results. It doesn't mean it's easy to implement, right? It doesn't mean that it's the safest. It doesn't mean that it's most reliable, that it's the most consistent. We can also have something that's the most most consistent in terms of weight loss. Doesn't mean that it's the easiest, doesn't mean that it's the fastest, doesn't mean that it's the safest, it just means that it's the most consistent. And same thing with the safest. Just because something is the safest, doesn't mean that it's easy, doesn't mean that it produces fast results, doesn't mean. And so the point is that we're ultimately looking for the statement of superiority that we can claim. And not just claim it, but we wanna have the rationale to be able to support it. So if I said, like when I talk about the E5 method, I don't talk about it being the easiest because it's not the easiest, right? There are a lot of easier kind of methods for creating a marketing campaign out there, but this is certainly the most effective in terms of producing new buyers consistently at scale, right? It's not the easiest. I'm not gonna claim that it's the easiest, I'm not gonna talk about ease, right? Or anything like that. I'm gonna talk about how effective it is. I might also talk about other areas, like it's also the most reliable. And so, right, it's the most effective and the most reliable of all the other methods and back that up. But the point is that you've gotta claim your solution has to be superior in some area, right? In one or more areas. Now understand for what it's worth without kind of opening up a can of worms here, that you obviously know that the offer comes at the end of your marketing and sales message, right? Comes at the end, right? We're not, for the most part, right? Unless you've got a winning offer where you're leading with the offer. For most people, their offer comes at the end. Well, for most people that have an average offer, they, if they claim that it's the fastest and the easiest, that has to be proven. You have to prove that. You have to give evidence of that. You have to give support of that. You have to give rationale. But if I said this is the easiest, right? I could then say, well, the reason why is because, and then I can give rationale. And for what it's worth without getting into this other realm, when I say the reason why is because, well, what I say next after that becomes proof, becomes support for the claim that I just made that it's the easiest. If I said it's the fastest, then the reason why is because, right? It produces the fastest results. Like this is, this works faster to produce results than any other fitness method out there. And the reason why it works so quickly is because, boom, boom, boom, boom, boom, boom, boom. Then I'm gonna give the rationale, right? And so that rationale that comes after I say the reason why is because serves as the proof and the evidence to support the claim that I just made. So in this context, without going down this crazy path, I'm not talking about you having to have testimonials or case studies, those things are all wildly valuable for sure. I'm talking about giving rationale support, when you claim some aspect of superiority, you gotta be able to back it up. At a bare minimum, you have to be able to give a reason why. You have to be able to say the reason why is because, and then follow that up with an answer. If you can't give that explanation, or and or you don't have testimonials or case studies that prove it, then I wouldn't say it, right? Because you have no evidence. Even if it's true, right? It's not gonna be believable without evidence, right? Or even if you believe it's true, it's not gonna be believable without evidence, without support. So again, a tract of panacea, tract of solution is seen as superior to other options in some aspect. Doesn't have to be all, right? Does not have to be all in one or more areas. Next is, right? In an ideal world, it can be positioned or framed at least partially as done for you, right? So the closer you can get to some aspect being viewed as, right, the work here in this area has been done for you, or the majority of work has been done for you, or a lot of the work in this area has been done for you. This not only makes the offer, the solution that much more valuable because it's tied back to ease, right, work, again, what does the prospect need to do to get it, but it also increases confidence, which we're really gonna talk about here, increases confidence on the part of the prospect that they can't experience the results. See, right, like, let's say so, I said earlier, right, I'm not a handy guy, like me and tools, not good, right? Not good at all. And so, you know, like when in doubt, like, you know, my first response is like, take out a butter knife, right? And like figure it out, like we'll use a butter knife to, you know, like, no, me and tools, no good, no bueno. And so, right, like, so if I needed to, if I wanted to learn how to, I don't know, you know, replace my toilet bowl or something like that, which I don't attempt to do any of these things anymore, none, because I'm terrible at them. And even if I could pull it off, it would take me like three days to do it. And right, and like somebody said to me, like there were two kind of competing offers and two competing solutions. And one of the solutions involved, like me, watching videos, right? It's kind of like, if you watch these videos, right, even if somebody said, you only need a butter knife and a wrench, right? And I'm gonna show you, right, how to, how to, you know, change your toilet bowl. And then somebody else was, there was a way that they were able to, like, you know, they were able to send me, you know, two or three components or pieces that I would be able to use that were already done for me to just use, it gives me that much more confidence that the outcome is more likely, right? Right, so the more I have to do and execute on my own as the student, as the receiver, as the prospect, as the buyer, the more I have to rely on my own understanding, my own education, even if it's through your program, my own execution, the less confidence I have that I'll do it, right? Because this goes back to that whole idea of, they have to believe that it'll work, right? They have to believe that it's worked for others and they have to believe that it'll work for themselves, right? And so, you know, like, most people lack confidence on their ability to execute and do it right. And so that was also largely why I decided, even with this, with your winning offer, with this program that I wanted you to jump on one-on-one and see it done, like, and have it done, you know, have it done with you there live so you can see and understand the process and see it actual live and walk away, right? So that, right? Rather than me just say, I'm gonna teach you and then you go do it all on your own. Well, the reality is, is that many of you would think like, I'm gonna botch it, I'm not gonna remember everything, I'm gonna miss certain points, I'm gonna get one thing wrong, that one thing is gonna destroy the whole thing and the whole thing's gonna blow up. And so the closer you can get to framing it as done for you, right, a part of it, a piece of it, the more of it that you can frame that way, the more confidence we, the more confidence the prospect has. And so, and of course, regardless, right, the solution gives the prospect confidence in the outcome. And so ultimately really, this is kind of almost, you could make the case that this last bullet is really a result of many of the other things. And so the easier it is to implement, the quicker it is to implement, right, the more of it is that's framed as done for you, the more that its position is different from all the other difficult and tedious solutions and methods out there, the more confidence I have in the outcome, as well, coupled with proof and evidence. So the more proof and evidence I'm able to supply to support these things, these claims that I'm making, right, and all these things are claims. If I say, right, it produces a big result for you, right? Like it produces a big result, that's a claim, right, like anybody can say that. And many of you know that many of you are competing with people who are not familiar with it, many of you are competing with people that say a whole lot of things, right, and have zero proof for it, right? It's easy and quick to implement. That's a claim, right? It produces fast results. That's just a claim, anybody can say that, right? And you know, like for what it's worth, without going down a crazy rabbit hole, right? The reality is that the internet is the least trusted of all mediums of communication today. The internet is the least trusted of all communication mediums today. Why? Because people know that you can go online and say anything that is based on zero fact, right? Like nada, right? Now granted, today we've become skeptical as a society of a lot of other mediums that used to have a lot of credibility, but I can assure you that the internet is the least trusted of all mediums. And so the point is that you've got to recognize that your audience knows that anybody could say anything online and make any claim about anything. And that's why a big part of offer creation and a big part of even marketing in general, or especially marketing in general, is proof, is rationale, is giving a reason why, right? That's why one of the classics of direct response, one of the classic books, and it's very hard to find today. Oh my gosh, my mind just went blank on the, I think it's Kennedy, not Dan Kennedy.
