You got it, John E. Kennedy. That's the book, reason why advertising. This is why they all talked about, like Gary Benzavango talked about the value of the word because and how the word because is one of the most persuasive words in all of marketing and advertising. The reason why is because what comes after the word because is viewed as proof and evidence for what you just said before it, right? The value of something like reason why, right? This is, this is, you know, this works faster than other options. The reason why is because, right? That gives support. If I said this is easy, anybody can do this. The reason why is because it doesn't require ta ta ta ta ta da da da da da da da da da da da da da. Right? And so anything that you say after the reason why is because or after the word because serves as a proof point. Totally getting off on a tangent here. Hopefully that, that makes sense. Now the thing I already said this just to reiterate this that these two right here are really ultimately the money. And so right are ultimately the money. Like this is where ultimately the easier your solution is to use and the quicker it produces results, the more valuable it is, excuse me, in the eyes of your prospects. This is why a done for you, a real done for you. I mean, real like where I'm going to implement, I'm going to come, you know, like I'm going to, I'm going to do it all for you is, is the most expensive option, right? Is always the most expensive option, right? It's the most expensive option because it's perceived as and viewed as the most valuable by prospects, right? Because it requires, it's done, it's, it's done for me. So it's super easy, right? And if you can do it in a day, then like, right? Like it's, it's a no-brainer. And I think this is why I said that this is another point that I think Alex did a great job in his book of really, when he gave the example, I believe, if I remember correctly, I got, I got a very early copy of the book from him of, of like the gym to lose weight versus like surgery, right? Like surgery is super easy. Like, you know, like call it super easy and works right away. Like you go in, you go into the knife, slice, slice, you know, like whatever, light bulb suction, like boom, boom, you come out and you're, you know, you're, you're lighter with less fat on you and smaller waste, right? Whereas the gym is a lot more difficult to implement but produces slower results. The gym is 29 bucks a month or whatever and the surgery is 29,000, right? And so speed and ease, speed and ease, speed and ease. Now I want to give a couple of points here as it relates to what it is that I just said. Number one, it's not about saying it's easy if it's not. It's about engineering the solution to make it easy for the prospect. This is why I said that of the three levers or the three kind of legs of the winning offer, this one is requires the most, you know, umph to make it happen. Cause this requires really taking a good, hard, honest look at your solution. Like there are a lot of people like, look, let me be frank. Let me, let me kind of step back, you know, for one second with this, turn this off for one second and look you guys, look right at you guys. There are a lot of people that are just selling wildly unattractive solutions where like when, you know, whether it's a client that says to me, I'm thinking of rolling out XYZ and here's how I'm going to wrap it in an irresistible proposition. And then I say to them like, let's go back to the solution. Who would want that? Like that sounds terrible. You know what I mean? Like that sounds awful. Is there an easier way? Can we make it easier for them? Can we, can we make it faster for them? Like what, how can we do more for them to just make it easier to produce faster results? Right? Like that sounds like a terrible solution. And when you start with a terrible solution, right? Here is even, this is an important point for you to understand. When you start with an unattractive solution or a solution that's not viewed as attractive by your marketplace, even with a sin proposition and the right presentation of flow and language, you're going to suffer. This is why I said that it's the three legs. It's the three components of winning offer together that work to produce the results. And so if you're trying to sell, you know, frankly, an ugly solution and you're trying to sell it with an irresistible proposition or a sin proposition and the right languaging, you're making the job that much more difficult for yourself. Because again, remember, what does the prospect get? And what do they need to give to get it? Part of what they give is the effort, the time, the commitment, the discomfort, all of those things that are directly linked to the quality of the solution. Really important that you guys grasp that. And so let's go back over here. And so let's talk about this. So I want to talk about those two things. I want to talk about ease and speed. I said already, right? It's not about just saying it's easy. This is not about just lying, right? Like this is not, right? We're not, yes, there are a lot of people out there that just lie, that just say what they need to say to make the sale. That's not what we're doing here. That's not what we're about. That's not what I've ever been about. That's not what I represent. That's not what I want you to represent. This is not about just saying it's easy if it's not. Now, if it is easy, right? And you're not saying it's easy, well, then, you know, shame on us for not getting that in there, right? But this is not about taking something that's difficult and just saying that it's easy. What this is about is looking at, taking a good, hard, honest look at your solution and asking yourself, is this easy based on their standards? Could I say this is easy and back it up with an explanation reason why to demonstrate that? And so if not, this is about us being honest about the quality of our solution and then saying, well, how can I make this easier? What are the things that we can do to make this easier? What are the things that we can add to the offer to make it easier, right? The bonuses or what other additional deliverables which we're gonna talk about, can I add to this in order to make it easier? As it relates to demonstrating speed, we kind of covered this already. So, right, how fast will I see the transformation? So like I said earlier, this doesn't have to be the complete transformation. So if you think about the journey, when I said earlier, I said, right, that ultimately what we're saying is that, right, to the prospect is that you're here right now, you wanna be here, I could take you from here to here, I've got a different way to take you from here to here and that different way is superior to all the other options in these areas. And let me explain why and how this thing works to get you from here to here. Well, this demonstration of the transformation, it doesn't have to be this whole thing, right? It doesn't have to be the whole journey. It's not just a matter of how fast do they go from here to here, right? It could be how quickly does it begin working? When will I begin seeing results? And so while if I had a weight loss program, if I was marketing and offering a weight loss program, while I might, and I was, this is for people that wanna lose 100 pounds or more, while I wouldn't, I can't promise that that 100 pounds is gonna come off in some timeframe that I know prospects are excited about, right? Like no one's gonna get excited about 100 pounds in the next 15 months, right? Like no one's gonna be excited about that, but right, when does it begin, how quickly does it begin working? Well, I know that this program could begin working the same day. Therefore, I can talk about that. How quickly will I begin seeing results? They might begin seeing results in the first 48 hours. They might begin seeing results in the first week, right? And so I can look at that timeframe to talk about speed, right? So there is a lot of things, just like, right? You could promise people that they will get 1,000 Facebook fans a month, or that they'll get 1,000 Facebook fans, or 10,000 or whatever the number is today that people are promising, right? But the reality is, is that if people could start to see new fans coming into their page or their group or whatever, in the first 24 hours, then that's what I'm gonna focus on. So I'm not gonna focus on the final step or the final step in the journey. I'm gonna focus on the speed at which it begins working, the speed at which they begin seeing results, right? I might say, you know, you could begin getting new fans, right, within an hour after you complete this program, right? And you can start seeing new people come in, right? Every single day, starting the next day, and then the day after, and the day after, and the day after, you can literally be building your group in, in, right, in a matter of hours after going through this. So, Nick, pop that question into the Q&A section, and when I get to Q&A, I'll take that for you. And so this is important, right? Because I want you to understand that if your thing works slowly to produce the final result, that doesn't mean that you can't demonstrate an aspect of speed in another area. You can, again, how quickly does it begin working? When will they begin seeing results? How fast will I see the transformation is really the biggie, if you will. And so all of that, what we just covered, is the first component of a winning offer, having that attractive solution. We want to start with that. And only after we believe that we have an attractive solution do we want to go on to the second component. And the second component is what we refer to as a sin proposition. So basically the way to think about this generally is that we want to start with the most attractive solution possible. Once we've got the most attractive solution possible, then we want to wrap it in this sin proposition. Now, ultimately, what we're doing is saying, I've got this attractive solution. Now let me tell you what the proposition is for you to get this attractive solution. That's what we're talking about here. Now, sin, as you heard me say earlier, sin stands for superior, irresistible, no-brainer. Now I want to break this down just a little bit, as you can see on the slide here for you so that you understand where these things are coming from. So when I said earlier, superior. Superior to what? Superior to competitors. So we want to have a better offer, right? A better offer than your competitors. Our aim is to put together an offer that your marketplace would view as much more attractive. I should say a proposition. So we're starting with an attractive solution. Now we're going to just put that attractive solution aside. So, okay, we've got this. What we think is a wildly attractive solution. Now what we're going to do is we're going to put together a superior, irresistible proposition that makes it that much easier for prospects to say yes to get the solution. So while I said earlier that an unattractive solution with a sin proposition will struggle, an attractive solution with an ordinary offer can do okay, can do well, but an attractive solution with a sin proposition will go like gangbusters. So an attractive solution with a sin proposition gangbusters. So superior, we want to put together an engineer, a superior offer to competitors. Now as you're going to see in just a little bit, that might be superior in one or more areas. Doesn't mean that it's going to be superior in all areas. I'm going to show you how to make it superior. It could be in one or more areas, one or more of the components of a sin proposition. Next is irresistible. And I want you to start to think about irresistible in a different light. What makes your proposition irresistible is not the volume of things that you give people, not the volume of deliverables, not the volume of videos, not the volume of Q and A calls, not all the stuff they get, not the thousand page binder or whatever that you send them in the mail, right? There was a time and a place, for those of you that are, I'm talking now in this example, I'm talking about, let's say, in the information marketing business. And this applies to every business, but there was a time in the information marketing world, the coaching training world where there was this, what was called the thud factor, where, right, this is when we used to mail courses and trainings physically. So my very first information product was going back, many of you who knows, some of you could have been in elementary school at that point, was a physical product that it was this huge thing came in a box and it was a thud factor. When the delivery due, the UPS guy put the thing down, it was a thud was the idea, right? When they opened it, there were all these pieces and components and whatnot. The thud factor. Well, the reality is, is that that was valuable then, that doesn't hold value today. In fact, in some cases, that is a turnoff, that can be a detriment in the sales process. The reason why is because years ago, when I first started, there wasn't the accessibility of information that there is today. And so, right, today, the reality is, is that information is plentiful. It is all over the place. There's no shortage of information. What people want, yeah, Sarkis, exactly. That was so, John, when John mailed the full traffic secrets, the original traffic secrets, that bad boy had all those manuals that came with all the DVDs and everything. That was the thud factor. But again, that was at a time when information wasn't nearly as accessible. And so, there was value in merely getting more information. Today, that's not the case anymore. The reality is that information is everywhere. And what people want is, right, they don't value more information because people are already overwhelmed with information. People already have information overload. What people want, right, is what's more valuable today is the result. The reality is, right, you could sell, if there was the answer to, you know, like the answer to some massive problem that a marketplace had, right, we could sell it on an index card. And if we gave just a sin proposition, made it a no brainer, like said to them, don't even, you know, you don't even have to pay me today. I'll send you the postcard and I'll, you know, use it and like see blah, blah, blah, the results in 30 days. And only if you're blown away by the results, pay anything. And if you're not, let me know. And I'll actually send you a check for a hundred bucks. Like the reality is, is that people would buy it all day long. And so, right, so there's that. But more importantly, what I want you to understand, the mindset shift that I want you to have is that the, the value, what makes it irresistible is the demonstration of benefits. So you remember the slide earlier where I showed you the scale and I said, our job is to tilt the scale, the value proposition scale in favor of what the prospect gets so that the value of what they get is far, is far greater than, than what they need to do to get it. Well, the way that we stack up the value proposition is by talking about what the prospect cares about most, why the prospect is even responding in the first place. And that, excuse me, and that is the demonstration of benefits. And so I'm going to teach you a process for, for sharing the benefits of your deliverables in a little bit. I'm going to share the three, the three layers and I'm going to show you the flow and the process of doing it. But it's the demonstration of benefits that makes it irresistible. When you stack benefit on top of benefit and you show your prospect, you stand to gain this and you'll experience this and you'll see this and you'll enjoy this and you'll no longer feel this pain, you'll no longer struggle with this disappointment and you'll enjoy this and this and this and this. What you're doing is you're stacking the value in favor of what they get and sliding it against the, what they need to do to get it. And so you need to understand that your offer becomes irresistible when it's loaded with benefits, not when it's loaded with deliverables. More deliverables don't make it more valuable. And some for some, in some markets, it actually works in reverse. You make it irresistible when you show them, you're getting all of this benefit for this little bit of money, work, effort and time. Right? Like you're getting all of this benefit for this little work, right? Effort and time. And that's why the reality is, is that the more work and effort and energy and time that's required, the more benefit we need to have, the bigger the payoff, the bigger the result, the bigger the outcome, the bigger the transformation, the more we have to have to compensate for that. But the point is that you don't load your offer with deliverables to make it more irresistible. You load it with benefits. You load it with benefits. I'll tell you, I've said this, I've been saying this for years and I'll say it again, I've never seen a campaign not work because it had too many benefits. I've only seen a campaign not work because it didn't have enough benefits. I have never seen too many benefits in a campaign cause it not to work ever. And so again, you got to recognize this mindset of that when you go into the offer presentation, which we're going to talk about in a little bit, that doesn't mean it's time to, you know, feature dump to start to talk about, you get these videos and you get the community and you get this and you get this, I'm gonna send you this, I'm gonna send you everything I got, man. I'm gonna send you all this, this, this, this, this. No, no, no, no. It's benefits. It's you're gonna get this so that you can do this and so that enjoyed this and so you'll experience this and so that you can blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, and you're gonna get this so that you can, so that you do this, so that you can experience this so that you enjoy this. So the focus is, You're gonna see we're gonna break down the percentage needs to be on the benefits. And then no brainer. We manipulate the no brainer with terms, which we're gonna talk about in just a little bit and risk reversal. And so the terms not price, the terms and risk reversal, That's how you make it a no brainer, right? Like, you know, terms, terms are how the payment is made. And we're gonna talk about this. We're gonna cover this. Terms are how the payment is made, right? Not about what the price is, it's how the payment is made. So if you're charging a thousand bucks, well, is that a thousand bucks upfront? Is that a thousand bucks in 30 days? Is that four payments, a 250? Is that a payment of $10 today and then 990 in six weeks? Like what are the terms, right? And terms, as you're gonna see in just a little bit, is a significantly bigger needle mover than is priced today. Because people are accustomed to paying things off in terms of payment plans, in terms of payment plans. And so people no longer even think in terms of, in price, they think in terms. And so, right, we could have, right, like pay nothing today, right, use it for the next 30 days. And only if you're blown away by the results, do you pay anything. And if not, you let me know and I will send you $100 out of my pocket. That's pretty hard to resist that. You pay nothing today, I'm gonna send it to you, enjoy it for the next 30 days, see the results that it produces. It either produces the results in the next 30 days, or you let me know and I'll send you a check for a hundred bucks and you can still keep the thing. That's a tough offer to refuse, right? In terms of being, like, doesn't take a whole lot of thought. This is my buddy Clayton Makepeace, a player, a copywriter, unfortunately, no longer with us. But he used to talk about offers, like, you take a, like, look, I'm gonna show you, I'm kind of giving you something that I probably should say in another 20 slides. But if you take an attractive solution, right, we make, we propose it or offer it to our prospects with something with an offer that's superior to the other offers out there. We layer on and stack on the benefits. We make sure that we share with them all the different ways that this thing is gonna improve their life, change their life for the positive. Then we make it a no-brainer, by manipulating the terms and risk reversal. Clayton said, he used to talk about offers like that as he would say, that's not an offer, that's an IQ test. Right, like, it's not an offer, that's an IQ test. And so that's ultimately what we are after. And so superior, irresistible, no-brainer. So let me tell you the six components of a proposition. We already mentioned it, we already talked about it, you're probably already familiar with it. This is where everybody goes when they think of an offer. And then what we're gonna do is we're gonna break each down. So there are the deliverables. This is what they get. So right, if you have an information product, this is videos, this is, you know, audios, this is a Facebook group, this is live Q&A calls or whatever, this is the record, like whatever it is that they get, the deliverables. Then there are benefits. Now we're gonna talk about these separately because of how critical these are and how important these are, we're gonna break this out, but there are the benefits of the deliverables. Then there are premiums and bonuses. There's the price, there is terms, and there's risk reversal. So deliverables, benefits, premiums, bonuses, price, terms, and risk reversal. These are the six components that make up any proposition. Now somebody might say, well, I don't have risk reversal, then there is none, right? But that's still a component, like there's no guarantee, right, the terms are you pay today, the price is 49 bucks, there are zero bonuses, right? These are the three benefits and this is what you get. Like that's, you know, that's the standard offer. You get this, this, this, this, and this, right? It comes with these two bonuses, right? The price is X, you pay it today and there's a 30 day guarantee or whatever, that's your typical proposition. So let's talk about the first one, which is deliverables. Now again, I said that there are six, or do this, six components that make up a proposition. You can see in the upper right hand corner, there's the number one, this way you know where we're at in the six, in case the word deliverables isn't at the top of the slide on any of these upcoming slides. So I want you to start to really pay attention to what I'm saying here because we're gonna get into some like do this, don't do this kind of stuff. So number one, each deliverable should address a different benefit, overcome a different concern or resolve a different problem. In other words, and you're gonna see this in a second I gave, I put an example slide on here. In other words, when you add something to an offer, when you add a deliverable to a solution, even if you add a bonus, and this really does apply to a bonus as well, if you're adding a bonus, bonuses are a little bit different which we're gonna talk about, but each deliverable should address a different benefit, overcome a different problem, excuse me, concern, or resolve a different problem. In other words, what you're trying to do here is you are trying to prevent doubling up with just additional deliverables that don't add anything to the offer. When I say don't add anything to the offer, by add to the offer, I mean add a new benefit, overcome a concern or resolve a problem. So you might be adding a third or fourth or fifth or sixth or seventh or eighth or ninth, 10th deliverable, whatever it may be, but are you adding an additional benefit? Are you adding the resolution of a different concern? Are you adding another resolution of a problem? Because if not, all you're doing is just doubling up on or sometimes tripling up on deliverables. So most of the time, when you see these folks that will just load up their offer with just a bunch of stuff, they've got so much stuff that just doesn't, it doesn't add anything new, it's just more for more sake. Right, and so it's one thing to realize like, wow, I don't have a deliverable that addresses this benefit or this need or this concern. And so what deliverable could I add that will address that benefit, that need or that concern? That's one thing, that's a smart and strategic way to go about adding a deliverable. But the poorly done way of adding a deliverable is to just say, ah, let me add this, right? Like, ah, let me throw in this. Or I think that XYZ would make a cool addition to this. What you wanna do instead is you wanna start with how does this deliverable, right? Add a new benefit or overcome a different concern that the prospect has about getting success or experiencing success or resolve a different problem. And if you can't clearly see the answer to that, then don't add the deliverable. And I'm gonna give you an example or some examples of this on the next slide of how I view this and how I want you to begin to view this. Now, also each deliverable should have a benefit-driven name or title. And so I'm gonna show you this. I also added a slide for you with this. I see so many people that will say, plus you also get a weekly Q&A call. How many of you think that people are excited about? Yes, Q&A calls. I love Q&A calls. Sounds really exciting, right? Like no one wants a Q&A call or like a webinar, right? Like just like jump on a webinar. I'm gonna show you just different ways to think about this. And you don't need, this is not like copy skills. You just take rocket to be a rocket scientist to do this. Like go the distance. So my point is here is, and I want you to get this. I want you to listen to me. Please, this is critical for this little segment of what it is that we're talking about, your winning offer. And that is this, that go the distance, not only with how strategic you are with, what deliverables you add, what deliverables you cut, but go the distance with how you name your deliverables and how you frame them, right? How you name them and frame them. Because how you name them and frame them changes the perception or can change the perception, right? It's just like the title of a book, right? You've seen so many case studies likely, if you've been around anything, any length of time, a book that sold poorly with one title and then the title was changed and it's sold really, really well, right? Nothing else changed. The content of the book didn't change. The title changed, the positioning of it, the framing of it, right? Changed. And so you've got to recognize that there is enormous valuable value in how you name and how you frame your deliverables. So let's talk about this. So let's say, let's go back to the information, the training type of thing. You got videos. Well, what's the benefit of videos? You make the case that this is how you're gonna learn. This is how you're gonna gain the knowledge, right? This is how you're gonna gain mastery, like of this bad boy so that you can do this, right? Now, don't like, look, learning, knowledge, mastery, I wouldn't call those real benefits if we were really getting into the weeds here, but you get the point. Adding videos, what does it do? This is how they're going to learn, how they're gonna gain knowledge, how they're gonna gain mastery so that they can do X, Y, Z, right? Then we could make the case that worksheets, right? Worksheets are so that implementation is a no-brainer so that you can translate very easily your learning into action, into production, into results. Now, do you see before I continue, somebody say yes or no in the chat, you see the difference between, there's a reason why we have the worksheets. In other words, I'm able to articulate the reason for the worksheets, right? I didn't just add them because, I don't know, other people do it and so why not? What the heck? No, I added it because I can then say right now that implementation becomes brain dead, simple and straightforward. How about like cheat sheets? You could say speed and ease, right? Like now doing this going forward will be much easier for you and much quicker because you can just whip out these cheat sheets and blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, right? And you'll know exactly what to do. So the cheat sheets are not there just because they sound cool. The cheat sheets are there because it helps me or allows me to talk about speed and ease, something different. What about coaching? What would be, right? So if we added coaching to this, what do you think a benefit would be? Right? So if you said coaching, right? And sometimes I see this and this is an important point guys, right? This is an important point that sometimes people will say, right, videos so that, and I want you to listen to me, right? I want you to get this, okay? So people will say sometimes in their offer, this is in the presentation right now, the presentation leg because they don't know the presentation leg like you guys will, right? They'll say you get videos so that you can learn my XYZ system, right? And then, and we have coaching, right? To ensure that you're able to learn the most important parts of the XYZ system. Ew, no good, no bueno. Because now you just added another deliverable with the same benefit. And if anything now, by adding that additional deliverable, the coaching so that you, you know, so that you can be sure you're going to learn the key takeaways from the XYZ lesson, not only did you double up on the benefit, but you really took away the credibility from the videos. Because now, right, if the videos were that good, so are the videos not complete where I need coaching to make sure that I learn it? Either the videos do their job or they don't, right? And so now you take away the credibility of the videos, if that's how you position coaching. And so we're actually the support, so going back to coaching, so what is, right? So coaching, what is the, you could say, right? Support, feedback, confidence, all the above would be fine, right? So like the coaching, right? The benefit is that you're guaranteed that you don't make any mistakes, right? It's kind of very similar to, you know, carry that over to the creation session. You could, a big part of that is, right? Like the ease and speed, but it's also, you know, you could say answer difficult questions, I might say, right? So that you've got clarity on, clarity when faced with decisions about next steps or something like that, but the point is here, as you can see, right, is that thus far, we're addressing a different benefit. Now, this is important because this is important for two reasons. Number one, we're not adding fluff into the offer in terms of just excess deliverables that are doubling up on benefits. We're not doing anything in here that could take away the credibility from a prior deliverable. But the other thing that I want you to see is look at all of the different benefits that I can talk about, your learning and your knowledge and your mastery and what that'll do for you and what you can do with that. And when it comes to implementation, you'll be able to implement this thing, right? Like implement this thing immediately and quickly, right? Like, and going forward, anytime you wanna do this, right? It's gonna be brain dead simple and minutes for you to bang these things out. And with the coaching, you're gonna have confidence that you can, how about community? Let's say we added community. What's the benefit of community? What do you guys think? Benefit of community. Support, you could say support. Now look, this is really interesting, right? You could say support, access to like-minded people, knowledge sharing, so certain things, belonging, immediate support. So certain things, right? Like, so let's go back to, so let me see. So April had said, right? When I asked about the coaching, she said support and feedback, right? And so which great answer, cool answer. And so now, if we rolled out community and hypothetically with coaching, we said, right, support, well, now when we roll out community, I don't wanna double up and talk about support again, because I already did, I already have something for support. These things are either complete or they're not, right? So in other words, right? Now like coaching, you get support, right? And we have community for more support. Well, why would I need support? Why would I need more support? Is the coaching not enough support? Is the support that I'm gonna get from coaching inadequate? Like, I know that this might sound like nuance, right? But it's not because, right? There are these subtle things that subconsciously impact people's confidence in the prior deliverables. In other words, if you're telling me that there are two things for support, then I guess both, right, are necessary and one isn't good enough and one is incomplete, right? And so if that was the case, now hypothetically, and I want you to understand this, I want you to get this, okay? Let's say that, let's say that we had two deliverables, like coaching and community, and they were both there for support. Like that's, they were designed for support, right? Well, then when I give those deliverables, I'm gonna give them together. See, I'm gonna separate the deliverables, and this is important, listen to me. I'm gonna separate the deliverables based on the benefit. If there are two deliverables that deliver the same benefit, I'm gonna talk about them at the same time. I'm gonna say you also get coaching and community for support so that you can, right? So I'm not gonna separate them out. I'm not gonna talk about them as if they're two different deliverables, because even though they are two different deliverables, they lead to the same benefit. So I'm gonna talk about the, I'm gonna combine the deliverables when they are both leading to the same benefit, right? Ideally, I'm gonna have one deliverable for each benefit, right? Some deliverables might have more than one benefit, but I'm gonna have at least, I'm gonna have one deliverable for each benefit. If I'm talking, if two deliverables of the same benefit, I'm presenting them together. So I'm not, like, what you don't wanna do is you don't wanna go, so you get coaching, so you can get all the support you need, and blah, blah, blah, related to support, and blah, blah, blah, related to support, and blah, blah, blah, related to support. And you also get to community, which is also gonna give you support, and blah, blah, blah, about support. You don't wanna do that. You don't want to do that. What you wanna do is you wanna take those two, and you wanna combine them into one so that you're talking about those two as if you're really talking about one deliverable, right? You get the coaching and the community so that you support, and blah, blah, blah, about support, and blah, blah, blah, about support, and blah, blah, blah, about support. That's really important. Community, right? Maybe it's camaraderie, cheering. You have your own cheering session, you know, section. Motivation, excitement, camaraderie, new friends, whatever, any of the above, right? But now look, if you look on the left side, we've got the example deliverables, and you look on the right side, we've got just quick example kind of benefits or reasons why they're included. And that's another way for you to really look at it, right? These are the reasons why they are included. And you'll notice that, right? Like they're included because they add a different piece of value, a different benefit. They give the prospect something else. This is a beautiful thing when you're forming your deliverables this way because it allows me, right, to say, like, every time I add a deliverable, I know I'm adding another benefit. And so, man, I am thrilled. And you're gonna see why in just a little bit because, right, I know that now I get a benefit, benefit, benefit, benefit and you're gonna also get this, which is gonna get you benefit, benefit, benefit, benefit, benefit, and you also get this, which is gonna get you benefit, benefit, benefit, benefit, and you also get this, which is gonna give you benefit, benefit, benefit, benefit, and you also get this, which is gonna give you benefit, benefit, benefit, benefit, it's crazy. You get this, this, this, this. All these benefit. That's what I want. But see, that's what I want because of the amount of benefits that it stacks, not because of the amount of deliverables. And so really, really important. We talked about naming. So webinar could become a QuickStart implementation session. Right? I'd rather, you know, like QuickStart, at least convey some speed, implement, get stuff done, versus a webinar. Like a webinar is a commodity. A QuickStart implementation session is not. Videos, easy mastery library. Right? Like you get a set of videos. Well, that wants a set of videos. Excuse me, right? That wants a set of videos. You know what I mean? Like, it wants a set of videos, man. But easy mastery library is different. See how, and it's not, doesn't take wild copy. Like I just went, bang these out. But at least easy mastery library, number one, it doesn't sound like a commodity. Cause everybody else, like, okay, there are tons of programs that offer a set of videos or access to our videos. This is the easy mastery library that you get access to, which will give you X, Y, and Z so that you can element OP. Right? You get access to the QuickStart implementation session. Right? So that you can benefit, benefit, benefit, benefit. Right? Maybe instead of cheat sheets, right? You got simple get it done bundle. Maybe instead of a Q and A call, there's a clarity call. I don't know, right? Like get it done call, rapid start call, depending on what you're doing. But keep in mind also, by the way, for what it's worth, right? That two things, one, should have mentioned this earlier. What we are talking about here with this whole, you know, you're winning offer, really talking about a front end offer. We're talking about, you know, turning cold traffic into new buyers. We're not talking about a back end offer, right? We're not talking about it like this, like even this, this offer is really a back end offer. It only went to our house file. We didn't run any cold traffic to this. It didn't even go to our entire house file. And so, right? The other thing is, you'll notice that even in our messaging for this, I called it cheat sheets. I didn't call it simple get it done bundle. That's because of two things. One, because I've got the reputation and I've got the credibility. And so there are things that we don't need to do anymore. If I was starting out in a new marketplace, you better believe I would have done simple, get it done bundle or some variation of that. And, but so the point is that on a front end campaign, when people don't know you and you don't have the credibility, the authority, the reputation yet, you got to go the distance. You got to go the distance, deliverables, name, positioning, framing, et cetera. Now, and what we'll do is we're probably going to take a 15 minute, excuse me, we'll take another bio break in 15 minutes. So the next thing is that is the way that we present. I'm giving you an overview right here, an overview of how we present the deliverables. And so you're going to see this, you're going to see this kind of again in the presentation leg, right? So when we get to the presentation leg, right? Remember, three legs or three components. There's the attractive panacea. There is the sin proposition. And then there's the presentation. Right now we're inside of the sin proposition and we're talking about the deliverables. So I'm giving you a taste now of how these get presented. So the deliverable is what they get. This is when I am now communicating the deliverable, right? So I said, you get this, you get the whatever, quick start implementation session or whatever. So this is the format that we use. And I'm just going to, I'm going to speed through this to get to the example, cause I think the example make this clear. We do a deliverable feature, why? Deliverable feature, why? And then you're going to see in a second, we go deliverable feature, why benefits? Deliverable feature, why benefits? So deliverable, what they get, feature, fact about the product or the service or the deliverable, really, and then why the feature is valuable, beneficial, helpful, advantageous. Okay. So this is the format that we kind of use when we take a deliverable, it's DFI, right? B, DFI, B, DFI, B, DFI, B, DFI, B, DFI, B, boom. We stated all the deliverables. And so check this out. So you're going to see this in action. So let's imagine that we were a hotel and that we offered guests, you know, that came to conferences. If you maybe, or if you hosted your conference at our hotel, you got a complimentary massage, okay? So complimentary massage. That was one of the deliverables that you got. So you might get, you know, free drink tickets, you might get whatever, right? You might get free night stay, you might get a pass to Disneyland or whatever. One of the things that you get, one of the deliverables is a complimentary massage. So how would we present this? So remember, deliverable, free massage. What is a feature, a fact about the free massage? That it's with a certified massage therapist. The reason why? To ensure you enjoy a quality professional experience. Now we're going to go on to the benefits. Now you're going to see, I'm going to give you a complete example in just a second, but the deliverable, the free massage is a feature which is a fact about the deliverable with a certified massage therapist. Why that's valuable or meaningful or important or beneficial to the prospect, to ensure that you enjoy a quality professional experience. And then the benefits. Now with benefits, I want to be clear with benefits, right? We want to show how the prospect's desires and deep emotional needs can be met by your product, your deliverables, better, faster, easier than anything else. I really already said this, right? Like I really already said this, we turn your benefits into the fulfillment of core desires. Like this is all, this is stuff that I've said, I've said a variety of different ways throughout our time together. You want to focus on the benefits, on the results, on the real reasons why people are responding. You want to show how your product, how your deliverables give them that. That's really what all of this is saying. You want to show how your deliverables give the prospect the exact outcome result payoff that they want. That's ultimately at the heart of giving benefits. That's what we're talking about. What this will do for them. You have a deliverable, right? And we gave the deliverable feature, why? Benefit, what will this do for them? What will this change for them? What will this produce for them? What pain will this eliminate for them? Right? And so that's really the heart and soul. It's the easiest way to think about this. Now there are three types of benefits. For those of you that have been through E5 Acquisition Accelerator or E5 Masterclass, you know this probably like the back of your hand. There are three different types of benefits, three levels of benefit. And I want you to pay attention to me here because you're going to see how this carries through to the presentation of the winning offer in a little bit. There's what we call the functional benefit. That's what the deliverable does. Like it's the, right? Like it's what you think of when you think of a benefit. Like you'll lose weight, right? You'll wake up earlier, whatever. Like, you know, you'll make more money. It's what the actual deliverable does. You'll get more traffic. You'll be more organized. So it's what the actual deliverable does, right? Then there is the dimensionalized or what Michael is doing. The dimensionalized or what Michael likes to refer to Michael Drummond or E5, our lead E5 facilitator is what he refers to as experiential. Experiential, when we talk about an experiential benefit, I want you to listen to me here. This is where you just paint a picture of what experiencing the functional benefit will actually look like for the prospect. Like here's where you're bringing it from the vague and the general into a picture. In other words, so, you know, it's one thing to say to somebody, you know, you'll generate consistent sales. You'll generate consistent sales. That's a functional benefit, right? That's what this will do for you. You will generate consistent sales. But then what we wanna do is we wanna dimensionalize that. We wanna give it dimension. We wanna state it in a way where the prospect can picture it in their mind's eye. This is important for you to understand because when we paint a picture using experiential or dimensionalized benefits, right? And we allow the prospect to envision it in their mind's eye. That's when we trigger the buying emotions. In other words, what you've heard it said before that people buy out of emotion and then they rationalize their decision with logic. But what most people don't talk about is how and when and where our emotions triggered. In other words, how do you get prospects emotional, if you will? And what most marketers, I want you to listen to me, okay? This is worth its weight in gold if you get this. What most marketers think of when they think of getting people emotional is they think of saying things that are emotionally, that are emotional charged or use emotional words. Like, they think of merely saying the emotion, right? In their copy or in their message. But the reality is that, right? The emotions are a byproduct of the prospect being able to envision in their mind's eye the result that they want, the outcome they want. So what happens is, right? When they can picture it in their mind's eye, when they see it, right? Like when they see it, that's when they experience feelings. That's when they elicit that emotional response. That's how you get prospects emotional. By giving them a picture of the result they can picture in their mind's eye. It's that picturing in the mind's eye that makes them go, ooh, wow, that's it, man. That gets their heart beating a little quicker. Their blood pressure goes up, their eyes dialy. And so how do we do that? So we take the functional benefit and we give a real life example of what it could look like for the prospect. So I said, I gave you the example of you'll, you'll get consistent sales. Well, what is a real life potential picture? Like what would they experience? And that's why I think that Michael's definition or Michael's term, excuse me, of experiential is a great term, is a much better term than dimensionalized because experiential is really like, what will they experience? So, okay, so we say they will, right? They'll generate sales consistently, but what will they actually experience? Right? Well, you could say that every morning when they wake up and they check their merchant account, they'll see dozens of new sales notifications. That's a dimensionalized picture. That is an experiential benefit, right? So every morning, when you wake up and you check your merchant account statement, you'll see dozens of new sales and you'll see that every morning that you wake up something like that, right? Like that's an experiential benefit. That's not functional. Functional is a, this was something that I learned many years ago from A-list copywriter Clayton Meekpeace. He really drilled this into me. He talked about this idea that most copywriters, the average copywriters, the people, you know, the average marketers, they tend to focus on functional benefits. So you'll make more money, you'll have more in your bank account, you'll be making more cash, you'll be able to spend more, you'll be able to take nice vacations, right? All of these very functional benefits are all of these very vague benefits, benefits that like you'll enjoy more vacations. That's very hard to envision in your mind's eye, right? But if you were able to say like, you know, like, you know, next year, you know, you can travel, you know, travel to Italy, you know, Italy, Spain and Greece on three separate trips, flying first class and arrive to have a limo pick you up. That's an experiential, because that's what they could experience. That's a dimensionalized picture. It's a dimensionalized benefit, right? Because we're giving them something to see, something that right now in their mind's eye, they're seeing themselves in first class, they're seeing themselves walk off the plane and have, you know, a limousine holding up their name there, like, right? You know, like, they're, and that's what stimulates the emotions. And so, and then there is emotional. And this is where we would simply spell out the emotion that they will experience when they experience the dimensionalized benefit. So like, right? Like, how will somebody feel when they wake up, you know, like they'll wake up, you'll wake up every morning and see, you know, and when you check your merchant account statement, you'll see dozens of new sales and you'll be thrilled and excited about the new money that you now make every day while you sleep, right? So this is where we then, we then state. So what we're ultimately doing, you know, you feel validated, you're making a difference. Exactly, something like that, right? It's just an example. So we're giving this functional, experiential, emotional, right? And ultimately what we're doing is deliverable, right? Feature, why? Functional, experiential, emotional. So if you remember, right? Let's go back to the massage, to the complimentary massage, right? So here is the functional benefit. So remember we said free massage, the deliverable free massage and the feature was with a certified massage therapist and the why was so that you can enjoy a comfortable or whatever and, you know, professional experience, right? And the functional benefit, you have a soothing hour of relaxation and rejuvenation. Now, experiential, from the moment you sit up after your massage, you'll feel a deep sense of muscle warmth and loosening, emotional, and you'll feel proud of yourself for doing something healthy and positive for yourself, which is something you deserve anyway, right? And now here is deliverable, right? Deliverable, feature, why? And the three benefits. You get a free massage with a certified massage therapist to ensure that you enjoy a quality professional experience. So you have a soothing hour of relaxation and rejuvenation. From the moment you sit up after your massage, you'll feel a deep sense of muscle warmth and loosening, you'll feel proud of yourself for doing something healthy and positive for yourself, which is something you deserve anyway, right? Boom, next deliverable. That's a simplified example, right? Now, the more benefits that I have, the more benefits that I have, right? The more I'm gonna share and say, the more benefits that I have, the more I'm gonna share. And so let's go on to, we'll do a couple more. And then like I said, at 3.30, my time will take another, we'll take our final bio break. So for premiums, the way that I want you to think about this, and I just wanna give you my big overarching approach, with premiums, with premiums, so bonuses, premiums. So again, so a couple of things. So for bonuses and premiums, same thing, it depends, right? So some people refer to them as premiums, some people refer to them as bonuses. You wanna ultimately use or offer or include bonuses that fill in product gaps and or remove excuses for not buying. And so you don't wanna just add in bonuses to just add in bonuses. You wanna think about, right, what are the gaps in the product? What could I add in as a bonus that would make this easier to use that would help to produce faster results, that would address something that I didn't cover in the core product that maybe I do feel needs to be covered? Or what are the potential excuses, or I really should say reasons why a prospect might not buy a fear, a concern, won't I have to do this, won't I have to learn this, won't I have to have this? And how can I address that and overcome that with a bonus or a premium? And so I'm always thinking about, like I'm thinking about what product gaps could I fill in? What concerns, fears could the prospect have that could stop them from buying that I could address with the, that I could address with a bonus? Is there a new problem or a new issue that the core product could create for that I can address with a bonus? Like hypothetically, just as a weird wacky example, if you're gonna start making a boatload of money, some people could potentially say, well, geez, I'm not even gonna know, what about the tax implications of that? Well, if that was a legitimate fear for my audience, then I could have a bonus that addresses that. Like how to make sure that you bank, you keep most of your money and not get slammed with taxes. And so fill in product gaps, remove reasons why or overcome reasons why they wouldn't buy. Just like deliverables, give them a benefit driven name or title, benefit driven name or title. Always include a reason, give a reason why you're including them. So right, this is, right, you also get this, right? So that you can element, okay? Right, so that, right, you get this, right? The reason why is, right, you could even say, right, and these little languaging things don't matter at all, but right, you also get this. Reason why I included this was so that you can, right? So that you have the ability to, so that you don't have to fear, so that you can start, so that you can stop, so that you don't have to worry about, right? Like I'm gonna give you a reason why I'm including it. So don't just have a bonus that is like, plus you also get this, and there's no reason why you're including it. Give a justification and show the prospect how the, the bonus, how it is complimentary, how it works with the core deliverables to increase the value that they're gonna get, that they're gonna experience. Just like before, you gotta present your bonuses with feature Y functional experiential and emotional benefits. In other words, I like to say this, I've been saying this for years, I probably could come up with a better way to say this, but this is the reality. You wanna think about your bonuses strategically, you wanna make sure that you are using them, like you're using bonuses strategically, and you wanna quote unquote, sell them just as hard as you do the main product with the core deliverables. And so don't treat bonuses like an afterthought. I like to think about my bonuses like, I like to think about bonuses as, and this is the mindset that I would encourage you to have, this is the mindset that I try to take, is I try to ideally create bonuses that are worth as much, if not more as the core offer themselves. And so I want, like I'd love to have, if we have three bonuses or two bonuses, I'd love for any one of those bonuses to be worth as much as if not more than the core offer itself. I'd love to have bonuses that are equally as attractive, if not more attractive than the core offer. So I'm using the bonuses, I'm trying to have wildly valuable bonuses, not ordinary bonuses, like bonuses for bonuses sake produces nothing. People think there's a lot of new and novice marketers that think that, well, you know, most direct response marketers offer bonuses, therefore I'm gonna offer a bonus, and then they just offer bonuses. And they think that just having a bonus or two or three, or whatever it is, that it produces some magic. It is, what is the bonus? What does it do? What does it address? What does it provide? Like how compelling is the title, the name? What's the promise of it? And are you giving me the benefits so that I understand the value of this bonus? And that's the thing. The key is making sure that the prospect understands the value so that we're continuing to tilt that scale more and more, just stacking more value, more benefit, more payoff, more results, more outcomes for them. And then of course, multiple is better than, multiple bonuses are better than one. Multiple bonuses are better than one. I think, you know, you get to a point, we haven't seen any data on testing of, you know, like it's four, better than three. I think, look, for me, I like three, I think, and that could be just personal preference, but I wouldn't go with more than three. Like I think it gets, you know, like if you have to, if you feel the need to start to add many more bonuses, that's usually a good indicator that the core deliverables aren't good enough, and you're trying to prop up the value with more and more, more and more bonuses. And so, you know, we usually have anywhere from two to three, we almost never have more than three or usually never have more than three. And that's because really I want quality over quantity. I'd rather have two to three. You know, we usually don't go with one, we usually have two to three, but I want quality. I want people to look at any one of those bonuses and be like, dang, I'd buy this just for that bonus. That's ideally what we're trying for. That's the mindset that I have.
