Awesome, perfect. Thank you so much, Leslie. And so what we're gonna do is we are going to, now we're gonna talk about pricing models. And so thank you guys so much. Thank you, David. So we're gonna talk about pricing models. Excuse me. So I wanna, so number one, we're not gonna talk about specific pricing. And the reason why we're not gonna talk about specific pricing is because it's so market dependent. What is low ticket in one market could be mid ticket in another market and high ticket in another market. Right? What you should price a product at in one market as an entry product could be wildly different than another product. And so it's a lot more about really understanding based on your competitive examination what the market is accustomed to paying, what the market is used to paying to get a better understanding of ballpark. Now, I will also say this, that just because the market is accustomed to paying a certain amount, doesn't mean that you have to price your product based on that. That just kind of gives you this assurance that this is what they're accustomed to paying. And so if I stay within that ballpark, that I'm not doing anything out of the norm, but that does not mean that you have to stay within that ballpark. When I first created my very first information product, which is now going back two decades ago, everybody was selling these $39 like eBooks. And I came out with like a $390 home study kit and sold it and it was cool and it was great. And it was awesome. And so, and I didn't know any better. That's why like it wasn't like any genius. I had no idea what the heck I was doing. And so the point is that doing your competitive examination is a great way to understand what the marketplace is accustomed to if you want to kind of a safety zone, but it doesn't mean that you have to stay there. I do like as a comment before we go on, Michael, and I really got to, if I didn't mention it, I want to give credit where credit is due. Michael was the one that really said, that dimensionalized benefit is really experiential is much better. I love Michael's thing about the somewhere in here. I saw he said, if you want to create, if you are going to write a great benefit statement, you must pay the fee, F-E-E, functional, experiential, emotional. And I love that. I think that that's super smart. It's a great thing to really recognize that. I think I started to share. I don't remember if I finished it, but that Clayton, who really introduced me to this idea of these three layers, Clayton, an A-list copywriter, one of the greatest also to ever play the game, like Top Legends, talked about how the novices, the average copywriter is focused on functional and the top copywriter is focused on the experiential and the emotional. And so there's massive value in having those. That being said, I want to talk about pricing models. And this is kind of more high level than it is in the nitty gritty details, because like I said, pricing is different per marketplace. There are two core pricing models or approaches to, I don't want to say pricing, but to the money side of the proposition. And the first one is a hard offer. And the second one is a soft offer. A hard offer is the traditional offer. Is the traditional thing that you see in most retail establishments. It's typically what you see online, where it is the, you pay now, basically it is you pay upfront and then you get the product, right? And so it's 49, it's 99, it's 129, it's 297, it's 497, it's whatever it is, you pay now and then you get the product. A soft offer is some form of pay later. Now there's a lot of variations in the soft offer. There's really variations under both of these, which I'll show you in a second. But the soft offer, the way that I want you to think about it is the purest form of the soft offer is, you pay nothing today, right? You get the product, you get to enjoy the product and really only if you love the product in X number of days, weeks, months, whatever will you pay? And so that is the purest form of a soft offer. Of course, there are variations to that, right? There are variations to the soft offer where it might be pay nothing today and then pay the full amount in a month. It could be pay a dollar today and then pay the rest in a month. It could be pay 10 bucks today and then pay two payments of whatnot. But a pure soft offer is no money today. That doesn't necessarily mean no credit card. And this is an important point. I'm gonna share with you a freaking gem, like a gem in just a little bit. But it doesn't mean that you don't get a credit card. It means that nothing is processed, right? And so sometimes for some merchant accounts, people, they have to charge at least a dollar, they can't get the credit card, something like that. But these are the two core pricing models that use. And then there are variations underneath these. Then there is an installment offer, right? Which is more, is a payment plan. And even underneath a payment plan, you could have variations because what people think is that the payment plan with a payment plan, people think that is, well, take the full value of the offer, thousand bucks. And we wanna have four payments. And so it's 250 or 260 or whatever it is, right? But you don't have to do it that way. You certainly can do it that way. But there are also, you can front load it. So you can do, let's say it's a thousand bucks, right? You know, it's a thousand dollars, let's just say for total the price. Well, you could have an installment offer that is 500 today and then 250 in a month and 250 in another month. So you don't have to have, right? Just evenly distributed payments on an installment offer. You could also do it reverse, depending on the marketplace, depending on what you're selling. Like if you were selling a business growth, let's say for new entrepreneurs, or let's say you were selling a program on how to get into business, how to start your first business, make your first thousand bucks, and you were selling it for a thousand bucks, just to keep the math simple. Well, you might do it reverse. You might do it where it's $100 to get started, right? And then it is the payments escalate as they go. Like in other words, it's $100 and then it's 200 and then it's 400 and then it's the remaining 300, 500 or whatever it is. Like so you can have it escalating, meaning that, right? So the reason why I would do something like that in a business growth type of program is because then I can justify, I could say, right? So by the time you make your third payment or your second payment, you've already made your first thousand bucks, right? So this gives you the time to get up and running. So the point here is that there are a variety of ways to do or to structure an installment offer, right? And so a variety of ways. You're not stuck doing just a straight up, you know, for payments. And then of course there's what's typically referred to as continuity or a negative option. And so, right? Under a hard offer, under a soft offer, then you can have continuity tied to that. And so continuity might be like a hard offer continuity or a negative option, might be it's 50 bucks today. You get the product, right? And then every month going forward, you'll be billed 50 bucks until you decide to stop, you know, your subscription. Or it could be, you know, a soft offer, where, right? Like you pay nothing today, you get the first newsletter and then, right? Only if you love it will you pay your first 50 bucks in a month and then it's 50 bucks every month to continue receiving the newsletter. And so that's negative option. Now I will tell you that without question, the single biggest mover of conversions in the offer models is the soft offer. This, the soft offer has built the back of some of the biggest businesses in the world of direct response, especially in the early days. And so folks like Gary Halbert almost exclusively relied on soft offers. I would say 90% of his offers were soft offers. Gene Schwartz built monster businesses off the back of a soft offer. The reality is, is that a soft offer is wildly effective at increasing sales rate. You can multiply your sales conversion rate. The key is that most people are terrified of doing it for, you know, for two reasons. One of those reasons I'm gonna solve the other reason I can't solve. The most marketers are wildly terrified of, or offer creators are wildly terrified of going with a soft offer because number one, their product stinks, right? Or it's not good. And they're afraid that people aren't gonna get the value or see the value or enjoy the value. Or, you know, they're, in other words, they're basically, their promises are bigger than the payoff. Right? Like their promises are bigger than the payoff. And so they're terrified that the overwhelming majority of people aren't gonna pay. I can't do anything about that, right? And so in other words, like what I would say is if you're promising things that you're not gonna deliver on, then shame on you. You deserve what you get, right? The key is that like when you've got a great product that you know, like if more people are exposed to this, they're gonna love it. They're gonna fall in love with it. Does this mean that every single person that buys on a soft offer is gonna pay? No, it doesn't. But what happens here is, and I don't have the math here because I didn't wanna get too far into the weeds with this, but what happens here is like, like, look, you know, I wanna share something right now, deviate for one second. And so, and that is, you can see the light coming in my office from the top right around this time, the light comes in. So I never do videos around this time, typically because I don't have any on this window. These walls are huge and these windows are huge. I don't have anything on that top like oval window thing. And so now you're gonna see this ray of light come across my face and chest for the next 45 minutes. What was I gonna say? What was I saying? I just totally lost my train of thought. It's a halo. That's right. It's not the window at all. It's a halo and angelic. What was I saying? I was talking about, oh, this is what I was saying. I was gonna give you some math. I don't wanna get too far into the weeds with the math, but the way to look at it is, right? Most people think like, and listen to me, please, please, please, please, listen to me. You are not in the business of reducing refunds. When you are marketing, creating offers, you're not in the business of reducing refunds. Or really what I should say is, you're not in the business of suppressing refunds as much as you can. You're in the business of producing sales. And so the thing to understand is that what most people think is, or most people have this mindset of, I don't want my refund rate to go up. Like I'm afraid that my refund rate might go up from 10% to 12% or 13% or 14% or 15% or something like that. And on a soft offer, there's no refunds, but right, because you're not taking any money upfront. They think, right, I don't wanna increase my refund rate from 5%, from 10% to 12% or 13%. But if I told you that, that would triple your conversion rate. Well, then you'd be crazy not to do it. I'll take a 5% increase in my refund rate if that means I triple my conversion rate. So in other words, I'll allow my refund rate to go up. If that means that there is a direct correlation with an increase in sales conversion rate. And you will always convert more with a soft offer. I've never, never seen a soft offer, not significantly, significantly outpull a hard offer in the same campaign. And so, and you'll understand how to make that work in just a second, like a gem, a gem for you guys. And so that's the first reason, right? Like people are afraid, they're afraid to do it because they're afraid that their product isn't gonna be good and people are gonna, they're not gonna pay and they're afraid they're gonna get beat. And a lot of that is just these weird fears that people have and these weird concerns, they think that there are a lot of people out there trying to beat them. Like it's not the case. The reality though is if you don't have a good product or your product doesn't fulfill on your promises, then yeah, you are gonna have a lot of people that don't pay. Even in a case where you have, you know, like you double your refunds, if you double your refunds or right, if you double your refunds, but you triple your conversion rate, you're still plus, you're still positive. You're not in the business of trying to suppress refund rate. You're in the business of increasing sales conversion rate. Period. And so I would happily take a higher refund rate if it was far outproduced by the multiplied sales conversion rate. And so that's the first thing that I want you to understand. Most people are afraid of doing it because of the, because their product isn't good. The second reason is because they want the money right away. So in other words, they say, well, if I make a sale today, I want the money. I need the money to continue to do paid traffic or media buying or whatnot. Well, I'm gonna show you how easy it is to fix that. I'm gonna show you how the change in technology, like for you guys, it's not a change for me. It was a huge change. But I'm gonna show you how the change of technology wildly just has shifted the game and made soft offers even more viable today than it was for some of the legends of direct response. Today, a soft offer is wildly scalable because of what it is that we can do after the soft offer. And so next is, right? So you've got these models. The next thing that I want you to understand is that there is positioning or framing of the price. So you have the ability rather than to just quote a price to frame it, to give a reason for the price or to frame the offer, if you will, the pricing offer. So there's a charter offer. This is an early one-time discount. In other words, right? You just release a new product, right? For the next 30 days, you're giving charter, right? This is a charter deal. And so, right? This is a charter deal. Could be many, many moons ago, when I was in my early 20s, I used to work for a health club. Whenever we opened up a new health club, we always gave like the first 100 people, we had this charter membership, which was like a thousand bucks or something like that. And it was lifetime. You paid a thousand bucks one time and your membership, you had a membership for life and you didn't have to pay anything again. And so we had a hundred of those. And then we put those people, their names on a plaque, on the wall, like it was really cool. So that was how that offer was framed. There's an exclusive offer. It's only for certain folks. It could only be for certain folks on your list. Maybe it's only for buyers. It's only for people that bought a particular product, a particular program. There's a limited offer, which we're gonna talk about scarcity-driven, a limited time offer deadline, which really all offers should be limited time, deadline-driven, which we're gonna talk about, how to do that in an evergreen fashion. A one-time offer, where they're never gonna see the offer again. I don't use a whole lot of those. I know that there are a lot of marketers that will do upsells. They'll present it as a one-time offer. Many cases, it's not really a one-time offer. You can find that offer somewhere else. But a one-time offer is like, this is the only time that you're gonna see this offer and you won't see this offer anywhere else. Right? And so that's a one-time offer. There's a qualified offer application only and there's a combination of multiple where you can have this charter offer that is exclusive, it's limited, and it's limited time, right? And you're only gonna see it once. Like you could have a true combination of these things. The point in me sharing this with you is that you have the ability to frame it. And by framing it in this way, you frame it as something special, right? It's not just, it's 49 bucks, but this is 40, this is, right? It's either, it's charter offer, it's an exclusive offer, it's a limited time offer, it's a limited offer. It's, right? You have the ability to frame it. It's very similar to ultimately what it is that we're doing when we're naming our deliverables, naming our bonuses, right? And shifting from videos to Easy Mastery Library or something like that. We're shifting the frame, we're shifting the way that it is being viewed. And all of these have been and are used effectively. Next is, and this is also biggie, this is kind of simple and straightforward terms. I already talked about terms are how and when payment is made. And like I said, this is a significantly greater needle mover than is price. In other words, right? The reality is, is that when in doubt, before you discount, adjust the terms, play with the terms rather than the price. So don't default to the price, default to terms. So the point is like, you know, there are people that will say like, they'll offer like a, you know, let's say a $100 product or something like that. And then for whatever reason, and I hate this when I see this, I hate it, I hate it. Somebody says no to that offer, they'll say, well, right now you qualify for an instant discount, get it for only 75 bucks, right? But the reality is, is that what's a much smarter and much more effective move is to, is to give a two payments of 50 bucks or two payments of $55, something like that or three payments of $33 rather than adjusting the price. You can adjust the terms and radically change. I would tell you that the reality is, is that playing with the terms is the biggest needle mover when it comes to making your offer that much more attractive than anything else. Even more so than price, more so than risk reversal, more so than anything else, changing the terms. Like I said, in its purest form, going from a hard offer to a soft offer, wildly different move. That's why I said a soft offer will be a hard offer every time and do it by multiples. And so I'm not talking about going from a 3% conversion to a 3.5 or a 3% conversion to a four, you could go from a 3% conversion to an eight or nine or 10% conversion or more with a soft offer because you lower the barrier of entry. You make it brain dead simple for people to say yes. And what you also do, and you have to understand this, is that there's a psychological impact on prospects when you present a soft offer. Because what it conveys is that you believe in it, that you're willing to back up the claims and promises that you made by giving them the opportunity to see it, experience it, feel it, touch it before they ever even pay anything. And so for prospects, it's easier to say yes and it conveys this confidence that you have in the offer. And so that's why you see this multiplication effect in terms of sales conversion. Now the thing, the gem that I really want you to understand that I really want you to grasp is this. And you likely know where I'm going with this when I alluded to this earlier, but it's thanks to one click upsell technology, right? Soft offers today are wildly scalable. They're wildly scalable because the reality is like, look, and I hate to say it this way, but I'm gonna say it in a way that you need to hear to fully grasp this. And that is that, right? The reality is, is that you can look at the soft offer as getting the credit card, getting the buyer and the credit card. Once you've got the buyer and the credit card, we now have the ability to put them through a dialed upsell sequence. That could include a bump offer, that could include upsell one, that could include a downsell if they don't take it, it can include upsell two, it can include a downsell if they don't take upsell two, it could include upsell three. And so you have the ability to monetize. So I don't want you to think, if you're looking at the soft offer and you're thinking, I don't wanna do this because I want the money upfront, you're not recognizing that you will get money upfront. You'll get a lot more money from a lot more people. And because with the soft offer, we are multiplying the conversion rate, you're radically reducing the cost per acquisition. This is a conversation that this is a training in and of itself that I could do for probably two hours. But you have to understand that when you go from a 3% conversion to a 10%, let's just say, or an 8%, whatever the number is, let's call it a 9%, right? A 9%, so you go from a 3% to a 9%, you chop your cost per acquisition, the amount of money that it costs you to acquire a buyer by a third. You cut it down like you save 66% of your money, right? So because you're ultimately multiplying the amount of buyers that you're getting from the same ad spend. And so what happens is, right? If you spend a thousand bucks, you spend a thousand bucks on traffic, let's just say, or you invest a thousand bucks, let's hypothetically say you drive 100 people with that thousand bucks just to keep the math real simple. And you have with a hard offer, you have a 3% sales conversion rate. So that means that you acquire three buyers, right? That pay upfront, that pay you upfront. And so if you take that $1,000 and you divide it by three, that's $333 to acquire a buyer. In other words, $1,000 in ad spend, 100 visitors, three buyers on a 3%, right? So that thousand divided by three is $333 cost per acquisition. Well, now if you go to nine, right? Now what happens is you radically reduce the cost per acquisition from $333 down to something like 120 or something like that, down to 120. So now what that means is that, right? And just in this example, right? So from $333 down to like $120 or something like that, might even be a little bit less. Yeah, it is a little bit less, 110, something like that, 111, 111. Exactly, Robert, thank you, $111. Now you've just, you've cut your cost per acquisition down. Now, right now you can put those people through an upsell sequence, bump offer, and that's beyond the scope of this training, right? That's all about AOV. We have a different training on that if you're interested in that, but that's neither here nor there for right now, right? And so now you've cut your cost per acquisition down to 111, and now we have the ability to make up that difference, right? To get that with your upsell, your bump, your downsell, your second upsell, and so on. The difference now is that you've got nine buyers, instead when before you only had three. And so if you really want to understand the game, the game is now we have nine buyers to put into our back end and continue to monetize them, versus before we only had three. You made it harder for people to say yes to you, you got a higher cost per acquisition, and so you had less buyers. Now you make it easier for people to say yes, you multiply your buyers, right? You trim your cost per acquisition way, way, way down, and now you use your upsell sequence and whatnot to monetize those folks initially, and then now you have nine people, you have three times the amount of people to put into your back end. It is an incredible thing when you understand the dynamics and the arithmetic of the game. And that's really the reality is for what it's worth guys, we are in like this whole game of direct response, or this whole business of direct response is really all about right, psychology, communication, and math. Psychology, the psychology of who? Psychology of our prospect, right? Comes from the examination stage with prospects. Communication, this is ultimately what we're putting out in front of them, what we're saying to them, what we're offering them, is it resonating with them, is it all the benefits, is it all the things that they care about, right? And then it's arithmetic. And when you understand the arithmetic of a soft offer versus a hard offer, the game radically changes, radically changes. And because of one click upsell technology, which didn't exist when I first got started online at all, what we had to do to get upsells was a beast. People had to reenter their credit card, it was a pain in the butt. Today, right, like the reality is, is that 60% of your AOV, 60% of the value of a new buyer will come from the upsell sequence anyway. And so now you're just multiplying the amount of people you're getting, you're cutting the cost per acquisition way, way, way down, you're getting more buyers, like it's just, it's a beautiful thing when you really understand it. And so that's why I'm so bullish on soft offers because they can be a radical, a radical, radical game changer for you when it comes to terms. And like I said, terms are the single biggest needle mover in all of the six proposition components. And then we've got risk reversal. And the thing that I just wanna say very quickly about this, I don't wanna be labor this, but I want you to understand that there are a lot of risks or perceived risks that prospects have when they're buying. What most people, what most marketers think is that prospects only think about financial risks. There certainly is the risk of financial loss. I'm gonna buy this, it's not gonna work, it's not gonna deliver on the promise. But there's also depending on what it is that you're marketing and selling the loss of face, like the loss of reputation kind of thing. Like in other words, like I'm gonna buy this, my boss is gonna see it, I'm gonna be embarrassed by it, it's not gonna fulfill on it. I could, my reputation could take a hit, I could lose face with my spouse, with my family, with my whatever, right? Risk of loss of time, risk of inconvenience, risk of hassle or intimidation, risk of unsatisfactory results, risk of disappointment and frustration. These are all risks that your prospects inherently know they're taking on when they're buying from you. In an ideal world, right, in a perfect case, we would demonstrate through our marketing message and then ultimately the offer, how we're eliminating as many of these things as possible. So loss of time, loss of inconvenience, like we try to address these, right? Like risk of hassle or intimidation. Sometimes we'll say in copy again, depending on who it is that we're talking to, depending on the offer, depending on the kind of the circumstance, we might say, right, like you won't get a phone call from anybody, you won't be pressured into buying anything else, right? That's addressing risk of hassle or intimidation, risk of convenience, we might say, right, on like on the guarantee, which we're gonna talk about, send one email and you'll get all of your money back. No questions asked, no hoops jumped through, no waiting a long period of time. That's the idea of risk of inconvenience, right? Like risk of loss of time, right? So risk of loss of face, right, is directly tied to risk of unsatisfactory results, risk of disappointment and frustration. This is about us proving, giving the rationale, the proof, the evidence of why they should feel comfortable moving forward. We're not gonna eliminate all of these risks. We're not gonna like, we're not gonna remove all of these risks, like 100% of them, cause there's always risk, but we wanna demonstrate how we are eliminating or I should really say, decreasing as much risk as possible. Now with risk reversal, there are also some core types of risk reversal propositions. Really the better way to really talk about this is guarantees, right? And so there, this is, we really could say guarantees. There is the traditional unconditional. And so this is when you see like, you've got 30 days and if for any reason or no reason whatsoever, you're not thrilled, send one email and you'll get all of your money back, no questions asked, no delay, no nonsense. That's the unconditional, right? That's the traditional guarantee. Now I am a strong proponent of always including an unconditional guarantee in your offers. Like I do not believe that I wouldn't say that we've got a winning offer that all winning offers have an unconditional component. Then there is conditional. And conditional is exactly what it says. It is where there are conditions that the prospect needs to meet in order to qualify for the discount, excuse me, for the refund. Now I'm gonna come back to this in just one second because I wanna give you kind of some subcategories that apply to both of these. There's money back plus, you'll get your money back plus, you'll get an additional, you'll get whatever, an additional $100 or plus, you'll get your money back plus you can, plus you'll get a gift card to XYZ, you get your money back, plus we'll give you additional coaching, right? So there is money back plus. There's double your money back. There's performance base, which we're gonna talk about in just a second. So this is performance based is basically in a nutshell where you either see XYZ result or you get your money back. So you either lose 10 pounds of fat in the first two weeks or you get your money back, right? That's performance based. There is time based, of course, which is, right, typically for the next 30 days, the next 60 days, the next 90 days, the next year, there's lifetime, right? So you get, right? If any point ever you are not over the moon, thrilled with this program, let us know and we'll give you your money back, right? If at any point you're not thrilled, there is a competitor challenge. This is like, right? If you use a competitor's product, put this to the test and if you don't see, you know, XYZ improvement in results compared to our competitor, we'll give you your money back. Unique off the wall, we don't even need to talk about that. I don't use those at all. It was kind of like, I had a buddy years ago who did like a, it was almost a performance based, unique off the wall, like, you know, you either see XYZ results or, you know, or I'm gonna mail a, you know, a thousand dollar check to your favorite, like to your favorite hardware store or something like that, like something crazy off the wall kind of thing. And then there is, of course, you know, like, you get your money back and you can still keep the premiums and bonuses. And of course, then there is a combination where you can combine these things. You could have money back plus, keep premium, you know, money back plus a hundred dollars and you can still keep the premiums, right? The, and what are we doing here? We're trying to show them that like, we're lowering the risk. Worst case scenario, you get your money back and you still get to keep the premiums and bonuses and use them. You get your money back plus an extra hundred dollars. You can't lose on this. It either performs XYZ or you get it, or you walk away with an extra hundred dollars, right? And so ultimately what we're trying to do is, is show them that there's minimal risk because they stand to gain by this proposition, not just not lose, but in some money back plus, they stand to gain. And so like money back plus is a true risk reversal proposition because what I mean by that is that the risk is being reversed from the prospect to us. So in other words, money back plus, like you get your money back plus another hundred dollars, I'm taking the risk, right? So I could be out a hundred dollars. You stand to gain either way. It either works and you're happy and thrilled with the results or you get a hundred bucks. Either way, you walk away with more. I'm the one that's taking the risk. Same thing with, you could make the case with terms and a soft offer if there was mailing involved, like I'll front the shipping. You don't even have to pay the shipping right now. I'll ship you the product and you either love it or you could send it back empty, half empty, doesn't matter and you pay nothing and I'll even pay for shipping. That's a risk reversal proposition because you literally, you risk nothing. I'm taking all of the risk. And so the thing that I want you to understand here is that we're ultimately trying to put together the most aggressive, boldest guarantee that we can. That's gonna be individual for each of you, right? Like, because there are certain things that many of you might just be terrified to do, right, wrong or indifferent, whether you should be or you shouldn't be or not. But the aim is, as we're gonna talk about in just a second, is to put together the biggest, boldest, and most aggressive guarantee that you possibly can. Now I wanna go back and talk about the conditional guarantee because this is another secret weapon when used properly that can do wonders for your conversion rate. And so the number, first thing I wanna say is that when you use a conditional guarantee, you always wanna make it a second guarantee, second along with unconditional. Always wanna have an unconditional component, you do. If you're gonna use conditional, you have unconditional and conditional. So for example, you might have a two-part money back guarantee. Part number one is that if for any reason or no reason whatsoever over the next 30 days, you're not over the moon thrilled with the results, the impact, the change, the loss of weight, the way your abs look, you let us know, send one email and you'll get all your money back. No questions asked, no qualms, no hoops to jump through. Part number two would then be the conditional. Now the thing that I want you to understand is that the beauty of conditional, if you believe in your product and you believe that if somebody uses your product properly, that they will get the result and be thrilled, a conditional guarantee allows you to have a performance-based, outcome-based statement, like and you either see, you either have six pack of abs in 45 days from now, or you get your money back, plus I'm gonna pay for your gym membership for the year. I'm just using that as an example. All you need to do is boom, and then you would give the conditions. All you need to do is attend the gym three times a week for each week, sign in, attend three personal training sessions, and enter your weight every day into our online spreadsheet. And so in other words, it's conditional. So in other words, you qualify for that guarantee based on the completion of steps. Those steps should be steps related to them using and benefiting from the product, not like some outlandish, right? As long as you weigh yourself six times a day, every day for the next six months, not outlandish stuff that nobody's gonna do, right? All it is is getting them to actually use the product. So like we did this with, we had an earlier coaching program. We did this where it was, I think the price point was $2,000, and then we had a $4,000 guarantee where you were guaranteed to bank an additional $4,000 of new sales in the next 90 days. And if you didn't, we would give you your money back plus a thousand bucks, right, thousand bucks. All you had to do was, and then it was right is watch all the modules within the next two weeks. And we were able to track all that, attend each of the weekly coaching calls. We were able to track all that and enter in what it is that you're implementing, like each week at the end of the week in a private, a private Google document or something like that. And that's it. And so what it allowed us to do was, it allowed us to have this double guarantee. If for any reason or no reason whatsoever, you're not head over heels, thrilled and blown away by the impact on your business, the sales results, the growth that you're experiencing. Send us one email, you get all your money back, no questions asked, no qualms, no shenanigans. You also get the $6,000, whatever it was, $4,000 guarantee, right? Like as well, you're guaranteed to bring in an additional $4,000 in new sales over the next 60 days, right? If not, you either do or you get back your money plus an extra $1,000. All you need to do is boom, boom, boom, boom, boom, conditional. This is wildly, wildly effective when you have a product that you believe in and you know produces results for people when they follow it. And so it has a massive psychological impact on people. It allows you to state and convey a strong benefit and promise a result early in advance upfront. It also forces compliance with new buyers, new customers, clients, which is a great thing if you have a product that really works. And the reality is that, and this is just the brutal, unvarnished truth, is that most people will not follow through on all the three steps or the three conditions that they need to meet. And so I think we, like we sold, hundreds of that coaching program, hundreds of people into that coaching program over 18 months or something like that. We had one refund, one single refund of that additional $1,000. And so the reality is that when you combine this with everything else that we've talked about, you increase sales conversion rate that much more. You get that many more buyers. And so that is conditional. Here are some simple, like you wanna think about the language in, like is it 100% money back guaranteed, much stronger than 100% satisfaction? Is it no questions asked? Is no hassles, is it better than risk-free? Is it we'll buy it back? Is it try it before you decide? Right, so like that's the beauty of, like let's say a soft offer, or even like a, even like, look, and I want you to get this. I want you to think about this for one second, that even a try it before you decide, right? Is not the same as try it before you buy it, right? Try it before you buy is a soft offer. Try it before you decide could be a simple, a hard offer. Let's say you're selling something for 49 bucks. You could have the try it before you decide money back guarantee, which is how you position, listen to me, which is how you could position a simple, ordinary, unconditional guarantee, right? So a simple, ordinary, unconditional 30-day money back guarantee can be positioned as a try it before you decide. If you see that all of your competitors are using a standard 30-day money back guarantee, and you only wanna use a 30-day money back guarantee, maybe because your merchant account or just because of fears that you've got or whatever it is, well, what you can do to change the frame is instead of just calling it a 30-day money back guarantee, calling it a try it before you decide guarantee. Take the next 30 days. Get your product today. Take the next 30 days, use it, put it to the test, see all the results that it produces for you, and then decide if you wanna keep it. If not, you send one email to us, right? You'll get all your money back, no questions, no hassles, no shenanigans. Now you've just taken a 30-day, typical 30-day unconditional money back guarantee, and you've changed it into try before you decide, right? Now, all you're doing is just reframing. That's all you're doing is reframing it, right? But that slight reframe, if all of your competitors are using a standard 30-day money back guarantee, and you want it as well, that's a slight change in frame that allows that part of your proposition to stand out and pop. Try before you decide. You're not saying try it before you buy it. You're saying try it before you decide, meaning what you're ultimately saying is that you don't even have to decide now, right? Just say maybe, try it for the next 30 days. See what it does for you. See how it changes your abs. See how it burns fat. See how it drops your weight for you, and then decide. And you're either head over heels thrilled with the results, or you get all your money back, right? That's a try it before you decide. I already said, excuse me, use the biggest bull's guarantee possible. I would be thinking what I'd be willing to offer a risk reversal that your competitors would think is crazy, that they would think is crazy to use. And I would, as I already said, aim to engineer it so that prospects see your proposition as close to risk-free as possible. I already said benefit-oriented, exciting name. Now we're going on to the third part, and this is going to be quick. We're gonna go through this quick, because this is really straightforward. This is about presenting your, presenting the offer. So we talked about the attractive panacea, the attractive solution. We talked about the sin proposition, putting those two together. Now I'm gonna give you some of the insights, some of the key takeaways that you need with the presentation of the offer. First and foremost, I wanna talk about what I refer to as, and you could see, there are, we're gonna cover six steps here, not six slides, six steps here. There's what I call the simple segue. This is how do you go from the marketing argument to the offer presentation? Like how do you go from the body copy, the body of what you're saying into the offer? Well, I'm gonna show you this. You're also getting this as part of the construction bundle. I believe that this is already in the construction bundle. If not, it's gonna be at, it is in there. And so this is simple, right? At the end of the, at the end of the, like the segue into the offer. It's by now you see how and why. If you're talking about your unique mechanism, if you're talking about your method, your framework, your approach, your system, your algorithm, your formula, it's, you can see why the XYZ formula is so effective at what's the promise, at stripping body fat off, at getting you that six packs of, at giving you six pack of abs in such a short period of time. And you understand what makes it so powerful for, now we're gonna give a variation of the promise. And you understand what makes it so powerful for stripping off body fat so rapidly around your midsection. And that's why today I wanna give you the opportunity to put it to work for you with a unique product or service called, what's the product name? Called, you know, the whatever, the, the AB 101 system. Let me tell you what it'll do for you, how quickly it'll work and everything you get today. Boom. Now we've segwayed from the body of your marketing message into the presentation of the offer, right? And we're gonna start with now the, the proposition. And now what we're gonna go, we're gonna go to, we're gonna go to the framework that we talked about earlier. We're gonna go deliverable feature, why benefits proof. Deliverable feature, why benefits proof. You got five deliverables, you're gonna have five of these chunks. Deliverable feature, why benefit proof. Next one, deliver, you also get this. Deliverable feature, why benefit proof, right? You also get this. Deliverable future, why benefit proof. And not only that, you also get this. Deliverable feature, why benefit proof. Now the thing that I want you to understand here, I wanna reinforce what we said earlier, is that I want you to think about it like this. And don't hold this to this exact 80, 20%. The overwhelming majority of what you focus on, of what you are communicating is benefits. So don't look at this as, wow, there are five, you know, five parts here, therefore, right, like benefits are gonna get lost. Like it's deliverable is very short, feature short, why is short, right? Benefits. That's where I'm gonna focus. That's the most important part of the product presentation, is that we are unpacking the benefits, that we're sharing the benefits, the benefits specific to each of the deliverables. So you wanna think 80% benefits, 20% DFW, right? Like 20% DFW, 20% deliverable feature, why? 80% benefits, boom, boom, boom, boom, boom, boom, boom, right? Like you're gonna experience this, this, this, this, this, right, just like it has for Sheila from Boca Raton, who said this, and just like it has for Jake from Colorado, who said this, and you're also gonna get boom, deliverable, feature, why, benefit, so that you can, so that you will, so that you'll experience, so that you gain this, so that you'll have, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, just like it has for Jane, who did it, did it, did it, did it. You could see that, right, like 80% of our clients, here's a screenshot, boom, next deliverable, you get this, feature, right, why, chunk, chunk, chunk, don't overthink it, don't think you need this whiz bang, right? It's segue, boom, right, let me tell you what you get, right? It's called this product, the first piece that you get, the first component of this product is this, right? Deliverable, feature, why, benefit, proof, next, deliverable, feature, why, benefit, proof, benefits, is really what I should say, benefits. Next, when it comes to the price presentation, this is the way that we like, front end, gun to the head, for lack of a better phrase, we give the regular price, like the value price, right, not the, right, this is, you know, this is a value of $100, right? Today you're gonna get, you're gonna save, you know, whatever, 20%, right? The reason why you're getting the discount is, this is how we love to show new customers how well our stuff performs for them, and so this is our way to make it brain dead simple for you. And so today you get it for just $70, right? You're saving 30%, you're saving, you know, $30 in this case, right, and at $70, that's the equivalent of the price of a cup of coffee a day, right? And I'm just making this, like this is just, so it's regular price, it's the discount percentage, the rational, rational for the discount, the offer price, savings, price trivialization, boom, very simple. Now, a couple of things that I wanna point out here, couple of little shifts, if you are presenting a payment plan along with a paid in full, so if you are, if you're, when you have a payment plan and a paid in full, so you got two options, you got a thousand bucks or you got a payment plan, right, of let's say four payments of 250. Well, the very first thing that I would tell you to do is this, the payment plan total should never equal the paid in full if you're offering them at the same time. This is really critical. The payment plan total should never equal the paid in full price if you're offering them at the same time. If you're offering the payment plan later, as like, you know, like as part of your follow-up sequence or something like that, then you could have it be the same price, that's the same total as the paid in full. But if you're offering them at the same price, you do not want the payment plan total to be the same as the paid in full because you will drive people to the payment plan, right? Like why front $1,000 if I could just pay $1,000 over four payments? Like if there's no difference, if there's no quote unquote, you know, additional money that it's gonna cost me, then right, then you're gonna drive people to your payment plan. So at a bare minimum, right, at a bare minimum, you want the payment plan total to be more than the paid in full. Depending how much more depends on the total price. So right, like hypothetically, right, like if you were, you know, you could, let me give you an easy, you know, roughly 10, 15% more. It really depends on the price, because like, right, if you're selling something for $50 or you got a payment plan and it's 55, you're still gonna drive people to the payment plan. Like in that case, I would probably have it be a 20% increase in total price with the payment plan. But if let's say you're talking about, it's a thousand bucks or you could get a payment plan and the payments add up to, you know, 1,150, that's sufficient to move people to the paid in full. When the total price of the payment plan and paid in full are too close and you're presenting them at the same time, you will drive people to the payment plan. And you don't wanna do that, right? You ultimately wanna drive people to the paid in full. And so you want the difference between the total and the payment plan, you want the payment plan to be more total. The other thing though, is that you want to present, if you're doing this, let's say in a VSL, you don't wanna do it where you're presenting the paid in full first and then the payment plan, you wanna present the payment plan and then the paid in full. The reason is because of a slight shift in psychology. So if you present the, you don't wanna present it like this, a thousand bucks or four installments of 2.99 because what this looks like here is this looks like there is a penalty when you take the payment plan, right? Now, it's call it it's 300, it's 1200, it's almost 200 additional dollars. Like so I could pay a thousand bucks or four installments of like a total of 1200, right? Like in other words, it looks like a penalty. You wanna do it reverse where I could say it's 2.99 today and three monthly installments of 2.99 or save 196 bucks today with one payment of only a thousand bucks. So now I'm presenting the paid in full as a reward, right? The savings as a reward on a paid in full rather than a penalty on the payment plan, right? So you should see that. There is a difference in psychology. The psychological impact is the paid in full is a reward versus the payment plan being a penalty. And so right 2.99 today, then three monthly installments or you can save 196 bucks today with just one payment of only a thousand dollars. That's the difference. And so you wanna make sure that you present it this way, not the other way. The other thing, just a little thing that you'll notice is when possible, I try to use the word installments rather than the word payments. So rather than three monthly payments of 2.99, I'll say three monthly installments of 2.99. Might seem like a little nuance and maybe it is a little nuance, but I wanna use the word pay like in payment the least amount that I can. I might even say in some cases, right? Or save 196 with just one installment today of only a thousand bucks, right? It doesn't, right? Installment usually is tied to payment plans. And so more often than not, I'm saying one payment today, but I'll use installments when it refers to a payment plan, not monthly payments. And so, let me go here. So let's talk a little bit about, whoops, I did this already. So let's talk a little bit about risk reversal language. And so you might have heard in the way that I've articulated or said, maybe you didn't pick up on it, these risk reversal propositions, you wanna present the guarantee as a positive with a benefit, not a negative. So I remember seeing this commercial. I don't know, maybe you guys have heard of it, but it was a regular commercial. I don't know, maybe you guys saw this commercial. And I don't remember if it was an infomercial, I think it was a regular commercial. And it was like this blow up, it was something that you blew up with air, or maybe you blew up with air, your kids could play with it. And on this commercial, the kids were like playing with it in the backyard. And they said something like, it's virtually unbustable. Like it, right? And on the infomercial, on the commercial, they showed like a car driving over it or something. And I remember maybe like an elephant stepping on or some crazy thing, right? Which is a marketing tactic of dramatic demonstration. And they kept repeating like it's virtually unpoppable. Right? Like, and here's a car driving over it, and here's like an elephant stepping on it. And no, like your kid is gonna love this thing or whatever, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. And then what they proceeded to do was they rolled out a guarantee. And they said something like, if at any point, right, if at any point your XYZ thing pops or gets a hole in it, like we'll replace it, you know, with another unit free of charge or something like that. Well, they just totally took the wind out of the sales of that offer because you just spent all this time saying, this thing is virtually unpoppable. And then you followed it up with, but if it does pop, like, which one is it? Like, you know what I mean? Does it pop? Does it not pop? And so what you ultimately want to do is in your risk reversal language, you want to state things in the affirmative. And this is very easy. It just requires you having the right mindset to do this. You want to state it in the affirmative. I'd rather use my guarantee rather than to talk about negatives and to talk about what is, and to talk about if this fails and you don't succeed and you're unhappy or you're disappointed or you're frustrated. I'd rather talk about the positive. And so, right, and focus on benefits. Not if you're unhappy or don't get results, you get your money back, right? But you're either thrilled and blown away by the results or you get your money back, right? And like more often than not, I even spell out the results. So, right? Not again. And what most people do is if you're unhappy, if you're unhappy or you don't get results, you're not, you're not happy with the results that you've gotten. Simply let us know and you'll get all your money back. Well, why would you want to start to get into the possibility of being unhappy, the possibility of not getting it? The possibility of not getting results. I would much rather say you're either thrilled and blown away by the results. Absolutely stunned by how much trimmer and leaner your waste is and how your abs are popping or you get your money back, right? So, you're either thrilled. You're blown away by how fast this works to trim fat off your waste. You're absolutely thrilled with the results or you get your money back. That slight shift in language allows you again to keep the positive frame and to restate benefits rather than if you're unhappy or don't get results. Why would I want to talk about that? Why would you want to talk about that? You don't. And so, right? Here's another example. We provide the finest widgets in the world if you're not fully satisfied for any reason. If you're not fully satisfied for any reason, just return your, well, why would you say that? We provide the finest widgets in the world. You're either fully satisfied, head over heels thrilled with your new widget or just return your widget within, see the difference between those two? Same guarantee, right? Same risk reversal, ultimately proposition, but in the presentation of it, you are, you're talking about, you're either fully satisfied rather than if you're not fully satisfied, head over heels thrilled with your new widget. And again, just for emphasis on this, it's important I would spell out the benefit, right? So you're like, if the widget helps to, you know, if the widget helps them to sleep better at night, you're either head over heels thrilled with how much better you're sleeping at night, with how soundly you sleep at night, with how easy it is for you to fall asleep at night, with how refreshed you are when you wake up in the morning, or, right, or just send us one email and blah, blah, blah, blah. And so, so important. That's risk reversal presentation. Reason why, and so this is a high level, you know, a high level view here. This is high level view for this and the next one. So number one, you have to understand death is the delay of the set, excuse me, delay is the death of the sale. Delay is the death of the sale. You have to understand that the longer you give people to respond, the lower the likelihood of them responding. And so our aim is ultimately to drive people to respond now. Now, this is especially true with direct response marketing. In other words, right, like with direct response marketing, you want a response now because every day that goes by, every hour that goes by, that they don't respond, you lower the likelihood of them responding. Why? Because it's, they are at their peak emotional state. Once they, once they get to the offer, once they see the offer, think of it as a flame that we ignited. Well, once that flame goes down, right, or once that flame begins to die down, every hour that goes by, it begins to die down. Eventually it's extinguished and it's extinguished fairly quickly. And so the reality is, is that delay is the death of the sale. We want to try to eliminate that delay. And so what, what are we doing? We're ultimately giving them a reason to respond now. Now this is all. Languaging. This is all right presentation. This is not, I used to include this in the, um, in the components of a proposition, but they're not really components of a proposition. Well, this is ultimately in the presentation of what is the reason that we give somebody for responding right now. Now there are two, there are two ways or two core levers to, to pull. There's urgency and their scarcity. I'm going to keep this real simple because I don't want to get too deep into, um, into psychology with urgency. Right. One of the urgency and scarcity are not the same thing. Right. So urgency is about, um, is, uh, uh, uh, responding quickly. Like it's about timing. There's a reason to respond. Uh, like, like is about time scarcity is about volume. Right. Like another word scarcity. If there's only seven copies of something that is a scarce resource. Right. And so, um, uh, but if there is a deadline, a deadline is about urgency, but a deadline is not about scarcity. And so, right. You could have a four day deadline. Right. There's no, like the, that four day deadline, meaning I have four days to respond. If there's scarcity in the offer, meaning there's a limited quantity. If there's legitimately a limited quantity, then I don't know when that legit, that legitimately limited quantity is going to be sold out. It could be, it could sell out in the next four days. It could sell out in the next two days. It could sell out in the next hour. I don't know. So urgency, depending on the, the, the, the deadline, I'm going to get to the important point in a second. Um, urgency and scarcity are different. The thing that I want you to understand here is that I don't know what you're going to get. Um, I'm going to get to the important point in a second. Um, urgency and scarcity are different. The thing that I want you to understand here is that for both of these, I'm going to go back to urgency in a second. There must be a plausible rationale given for. If you're using scarcity, like there, right. If you have a legitimate scarcity, there's only a limited number of seats. There's only so much bandwidth that you have. There's only so many new clients you could take on. Right. Um, then we're going to use it. If there's not a legitimate scarcity, then do not use it. If you have a limited number of seats, you can we could just use it in a few seconds. If you have a limited number of seats, then you don't need it now. But you don't need it. You don't need it. But you will at a bare minimum rely on urgency in an ideal world. You want to leverage both. Both are the best example, like both are the best when used together. Like we run offers all the time where it is. There is a deadline and there's also a limited number of spots. And whichever comes first, that's when the offer comes down. Either when the limited number of spots are snatched up or when the I will tell you that right scarcity is scarcity creates more pressure or gives more of a reason to buy because with scarcity there is the element of unknown right if there are only 30 copies I have no idea how quickly these things will sell out they might not sell out for three days they might sell out in the next 30 minutes I don't know if you don't want to risk missing out right then you get it that's why scarcity has that added pressure above urgency urgency from a deadline you there are no unknowns you know what the deadline is the thing that I'm going to tell you guys the most important thing for me for you guys on here right is that every single offer should have a deadline I'll tell you that I've really known this for many many years and got lax with this just because things you know we're coming easy to us and reputation and trust and all and all that but every single offer should have a deadline I personally recommend and love deadline funnel right it's it is a and I think probably if Chris Cole still on here he can speak to the value of deadline funnel we use it every day all the time. All the time. And what deadline funnel does what's great about deadline funnel is number one is is this is it allows you to have legitimate. Evergreen deadlines for every single offer for each person so you can set up and you can have an evergreen winning offer an evergreen campaign with a winning offer in the end and build in build in a let's say a four day deadline and I wouldn't go too much further than four days. We tend to use four days for three to four days at the most. Because when you go longer it starts to act you know it starts to perform worse. You know like seven days you're there's really no urgency if I have a whole week to make a decision. But the beauty is is that what it does is it gives a real live deadline for each person coming into your campaign in real time. So it's not like a fixed date it's not like the traditional countdown timer where you know it let's say if we're counting down to September 4 and now everybody the offer expires on September 4 for everybody regardless of when they come in. What you can do with deadline funnel is you can have an evergreen campaign with a winning offer and everybody that hits the sales page no matter when they hit it has a four day window a four day deadline for each individual person doesn't mean if they come in today four days if they come in tomorrow four days if they come in on on Saturday four days they come in on Sunday four days. Right and so what it does is it allows it allows us to have these real live you know like evergreen campaigns that have a real legitimate deadline for each person that those people cannot go back and get it unless we redo the campaign like we relaunch the campaign so it's it's it works with cookies. It's deep but the point is that every single offer should have a deadline you will sell less without a deadline period if you don't give people a like like most of you for most of your offers you're not going to be able to leverage scarcity for most offers especially for those of you that want an evergreen front end campaign for an evergreen front end campaign you're not going to have scarcity right and like you're not going to have legitimate true scarcity. And so what you're going to need to use to give a reason to to respond is ultimately without getting into the psychology of the more advanced way of using urgency which is psychology based, which is more about, you know, articulating the speed of of the definition of problem and the speed of benefits and somebody you're going to rely on a deadline. And so you need a deadline. If you don't give people a reason to respond now most people will not. And this is why what we see today is we see with with campaigns, we see a hockey stick. We see a hockey stick meaning that the majority of people buy on the last day and the majority of people on the last day by in the last three hours. And that's the right and now imagine if you didn't have that deadline if you didn't have that reason to respond. Most people would not respond. And so if there's no reason why I have to respond right now it could be well this sounds great when I get to it when I have the free time when I write and so every single offer should have a deadline. I believe if you roll out a campaign without a deadline you are losing money you're leaving money on the table. Every single offer should have a deadline. No such thing as an offer that is just out there. It's always hurt us. And so I have a lot of campaigns that are out there that that when my team has bandwidth that will be coming down and put up with a deadline. The ones that have deadlines. Those are the ones that produce the highest conversion rates. Period. So at a bare minimum, a deadline. And again, I like deadline funnel because what we're talking about having here is an evergreen campaign that you can use with paid traffic every single day and still be authentic and honest in the deadline because you're saying that for that person they have four days, just like the next comes in the next day they have four days. And so it's it's honest, it's authentic, it's it's call it hard coded to to a contact record. And it expires doesn't mean that it expires for everybody on that day it's it's individualized. And so it is a, it's a beautiful thing. So, call to action, I want to give you a couple of little advanced tips here, these aren't critical, these aren't, you know, mandatory by any stretch of the imagination, call to action is really just all about telling somebody go ahead click the button below and get yours today right that's ultimately call to action. But these are some tricks, depending on your offer and depending on your marketplace depending on your solution that you can build in. So you can use sometimes the contrast technique, right which is where you're telling the prospect what they will experience if they don't respond. In other words, this is where we are amplifying the consequence of not responding. Right, so if I was talking to somebody that if I was, if I was marketing and selling and offering, let's say a diabetes supplement a supplement to help lower, you know, blood sugar. Well, I would certainly talk about, you know, like, you know, don't let your blood sugar get out of out of control, don't leave your blood sugar skyrocketing damaging your nerves potentially exposing you to paralysis impacting your eyesight you have the chance today to finally boom boom boom and then I'm going to go back to benefit. And so the contrast technique is basically talking about what the prospect stands to gain if they don't respond or what they what they stand to experience if they don't respond, and we're amplifying the consequences. This doesn't always apply to every business which is why it's not a hard and fast kind of rule as part of the the CTA for the offer. But when there is when there is a negative that stands to get worse if they don't respond, you most certainly can touch on that in the call to action part. Of course, I already said this a number of times, you ultimately want to focus on the on the result the outcome the transformation I've said this right the after state that they're here now they want to be here. As much as you can you want to focus on the transformation, you want to be spending the majority of your time, even in the call to action talking about right. Okay, go ahead, click the button below finally get the abs you want finally trim your ways finally feel great and confident on the beach when you take your shirt off go ahead click the button below and get shredded abs now whatever the product is is called and so again right re emphasizing the the transformation the outcome, the result. This is also again just a little sometimes you know when you want to you want to future pays right like, you know, when you're talking about, you know, you know, when your book arrives. So Leslie asked when competitors lie when competitors lie about the results and benefits of their product. How do I counter that they all say that their food provides complete nutrition I can explain point by point why their product is not provide this and my balance food plan and using my bird bird food as a foundational food does avian malnutrition is the leading cause of illness disease and early death of the of these amazing animals thanks. So this is a great question. And so it's a little bit of a tough question so because there's a little bit of nuance here. The first answer that I would really give right that I would really say is you asked the, how do I counter that right that they're lying, you don't. The reality is is that you really don't what you don't want to do is you don't want to spend time. You don't want to spend time talking about your competitors in in your marketing, it kind of takes away positioning and credibility. I certainly would never mention any of them by name and this goes for everybody on here I would never talk about a competitor by name negative positive doesn't matter. There are a lot of people out there that lie there's very little that we can do about that. Most of those people over time get exposed if you're promising the moon and you're delivering, you know, grass. It's only a matter of time before people see it and, and it starts to get out there. With that being said, I do think that in your case. What you can do is that when you are explaining how like why your food is complete or is balanced. What you can do is you can demonstrate that here's what makes this different from the other food providers. That use the word balanced in their in their food. And then what I would do is I would go on to demonstrate why yours is truly balanced without kind of coming out and saying they say theirs is balanced but theirs isn't really balanced ours is balanced they're lying we're not. You don't want to go down that route that there's a whole bunch of psychology about like the you know people tend to people tend to look negatively upon the messenger of bad news even if that bad news is not from the the like the messenger about the like even if that one even if that messenger is trying to help the situation the messenger delivering bad news tends to be viewed in a lower level than if they didn't deliver that that bad news and so you don't want to do that but I don't I think that you in your instance can get away can get away with talking about like you know while there while there are other there there are there's other food that uses the label of balanced. I want to explain what makes ours different and I want you to understand what truly balanced bird food is and requires and then you get to go into kind of education mode. If you will where ultimately your education is ebm content is education based marketing content, you know kind of creating more, more desire if you will for your food than theirs. And so hopefully that makes that makes sense. Leslie. Next is. So I mean asked how do you speed up the process of identifying competitors and their offering. There's really no way to speed it up. I mean the reality is is that you if you don't already know who your competitors are. The reality is is that for the most part with the type of competitive examination that we're doing, it is manual labor there is no simplified software to do that, but identifying competitors should, you know that that's should take you less than an hour to be able to identify competitors and any the top competitors in any marketplace. And so the beauty is that you just have to pivot if you had somebody who came along or multiple people who came along, and they've knocked you off in terms of the way that they're explaining how their product or service works or, you know, their their system works, and the way that they're explaining it is the same way that you're explaining it then you just have to look for a different way to explain it, you either have to look for a different way to explain it or you have to look for a different mech unique mechanism. So the USP so you know people most people talk about a USP unique selling proposition, and which is really a tool for differentiation. What people don't realize is that the majority like there was a time when they're developing a unique selling proposition was much easier than it is today today unique selling propositions are rare. A unique selling proposition is when you have a product or service that offers a unique benefit to the marketplace that no other competitor in the marketplace offers. So, like when you when your product or service offers a benefit, a unique benefit to the marketplace that no other competitors offer. That's when you have a unique selling proposition. And so the reality is is that today unique selling propositions are rare they're only seen typically with disruptive technologies like when uber first hit the scene and right like you're able to hail a ride, you know, a car from your phone. Today usps are extremely rare for the overwhelming majority of products and services out there, minus the disruptive technologies. And so this is why as a side note without I'm not going to do a pitch for e five for those of you who haven't gone through e five, e five a or e five master class, but the other way the only other way to meaningfully differentiate a in marketing, if you don't have a USP is with the um exactly what Robert said is with a unique mechanism, unique mechanism is is the is the single most effective tool for differentiation.
